issue_title: connection.whois() does not take single strings
issue_body:
The connection.whois() method should check to see whether the parameter it was passed was a string or a list and act accordingly.

With a string:
```
#!python
>>> "WHOIS " + ",".join( "JoshTheEnder" ) 
'WHOIS J,o,s,h,T,h,e,E,n,d,e,r' 
```
With a list (which i assume is how it's supposed to be done but seems pointless for 1 user):
```
#!python
>>> "WHOIS " + ",".join( ["JoshTheEnder"] )
'WHOIS JoshTheEnder' 
```

Suggestion: Making the function check the type of the argument and act accordingly between string and list objects

issue_title: Correct method for parsing proprietary sentences?
issue_body:
I have a Seapath MRU.  It outputs the following proprietary sentences:
```
$PSXN,20,horiz-qual,hgt-qual,head-qual,rp-qual*csum term
$PSXN,22,gyro-calib,gyro-offs*csum term
$PSXN,23,roll,pitch,head,heave*csum term
$PSXN,24,roll-rate,pitch-rate,yaw-rate,vertical-vel*csum term
$PSXN,21,event*csum term
```
where:
```
horiz-qual: Horizontal position and velocity quality: 0 = normal, 1 = reduced performance, 2= invalid data.
hgt-qual: Height and vertical velocity quality: 0 = normal, 1 = reduced performance, 2 =invalid data.
head-qual: Heading quality: 0 = normal, 1 = reduced performance, 2 = invalid data.
rp-qual: Roll and pitch quality: 0 = normal, 1 = reduced performance, 2 = invalid data.
gyro-calib: Gyro calibration value since system start-up in degrees on format d.dd.
gyro-offs: Short-term gyro offset in degrees on format d.dd.
roll: Roll in degrees on format d.dd. Positive with port side up.
pitch: Pitch in degrees on format d.dd. Positive with bow up.
heave: Heave in metres on format d.dd. Positive down.
roll-rate: Roll rate in degrees per second on format d.dd. Positive when port side is moving upwards.
pitch-rate: Pitch rate in degrees per second on format d.dd. Positive when bow is moving upwards.
yaw-rate: Yaw rate in degrees per second on format d.dd. Positive when bow is moving towards starboard.
vertical-vel: Vertical velocity in metres per second on format d.dd. Positive when moving downwards.
event: Event code: 1 = system restart.
csum: Checksum (exclusive or) of all characters between, but not including, the preceding $ and * , hexadecimal (00 - FF).
term: CR-LF (2 bytes, values 13 and 10).
```
with sample data:
```
'$PSXN,20,0,0,0,0*3B',
'$PSXN,23,0.30,-0.97,298.57,0.13*1B',
'$PSXN,26,0,44.5000,0.7800,-0.9000,NRP*6D',
```

So I created the following class:
```
class SXN(pynmea2.ProprietarySentence):
    def __init__(self, manufacturer, data):
        super(SXN, self).__init__(manufacturer, data)
        self.sentence_type = data[1]
        if self.sentence_type == '23':
            SXN.fields = (
                ('Empty', '_'),
                ('sentence', 's'),
                ('roll', 'roll'),
                ('pitch', 'pitch'),
                ('head', 'head'),
                ('heave', 'heave')
            )
            SXN.name_to_idx = dict((f[1], i) for i, f in enumerate(SXN.fields))
```

This seems to work for the $PSXN,23 message, but as I think about supporting the others it seems kludgey. Is there a better approach?

The doc should mention that the $SHELL env variable needs to be exported for bash

Using Cygwin, I had the `$SHELL` variable automatically defined but NOT exported (using the `export` command).
In that situation, `pew` isn't able to detect that I'm using `bash`, and hence launch a simple `sh` shell without loading my `.bashrc`:
https://github.com/berdario/pew/blob/master/pew/pew.py#L169
Adding `export SHELL` to my `.bashrc` solved the problem.

issue_title: Document exceptions raised by Kernel.run
issue_body:
It seems that curio.run() may raise a TaskError. This is currently not documented, the documentation for Kernel.run does not mention any exceptions, and the documentation for TaskError states that this is only raised by a taks's join() method.

It would be nice to document the current behavior.

It would be even nicer (in my opinion) to also just re-raise the original exception. I don't see much point in wrapping it in a TaskError. In particular, when unit-testing a program that uses curio curoutines, this makes working with the tests rather awkward.

issue_title: Why does will run non-daemonic threads vs. daemonic?
issue_body:
Hi, 

I've read a bit about how will bootstraps its threads and the first thing I've noticed (correct me if I'm wrong) is that it is running non-daemonic threads.  Is there a reason for using non-daemonic threads over daemonic ones?  

```
https://github.com/skoczen/will/blob/d074cb2b577264f0ab23215b3dd5cab590551abb/will/main.py#L111
```

I'm chasing a possible memory leak issue when will is run for a couple of days and I'm interested on how will cleans up itself after being killed abruptly (e.g. by an oom killer).

Cheers

issue_title: Keccak implementations missing when installed from pip
issue_body:
The following error comes up when trying to import `sha3` on Python 3.6, after using pip to install `pysha3` (both 1.0.0 and 1.0.1):

```python
Python 3.6.0 (default, Feb  2 2017, 12:13:44)
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 8.0.0 (clang-800.0.42.1)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import sha3
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/Brett/.pyenv/versions/bigchaindb-3.6/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sha3.py", line 8, in <module>
    from _sha3 import keccak_224, keccak_256, keccak_384, keccak_512
ImportError: cannot import name 'keccak_224'
```

Inspecting an imported `_sha3`:

```python
>>> import _sha3
>>> dir(_sha3)
['__doc__', '__file__', '__loader__', '__name__', '__package__', '__spec__', 'implementation', 'keccakopt', 'sha3_224', 'sha3_256', 'sha3_384', 'sha3_512', 'shake_128', 'shake_256']
```

(So everything but the keccak implementations is available)

To install, I used a normal pip install:

```bash
pip install pysha3
```

This happens on native OSX (10.11), macOS (10.12), and on a docker container running the [official 3.6 alpine image](https://hub.docker.com/_/python/).

I'm not sure what's going on, since it looks like the [`PY_WITH_KECCAK` flag is defined](https://github.com/tiran/pysha3/blob/master/setup.py#L76), but maybe it's not getting built with it?

issue_title: Slow response?
issue_body:
Good afternoon. You have a great library. But is it possible to accelerate the search as quickly as possible? Maybe I'm not doing something right. I compare ldap3 and bonsai in asynchronous mode. And the difference in the answer is huge.
```

"""
depend bonsai:
 apt-get install libsasl-dev,libldap2-dev,python3-dev,libkrb5-dev,libsasl2-dev

"""

from time import time
from asyncio import get_event_loop

from ldap3 import Server, ALL, Connection, ALL_ATTRIBUTES
from bonsai import LDAPClient

host, port = 'xxx', 389
user = 'cn=name,ou=user,dc=test,dc=wt'
pswd = 'test'

client = LDAPClient("ldap://{}:{}".format(host, port))
client.set_credentials("SIMPLE", (user, pswd))
dn = 'dc=test,dc=wt'
filter = 'cn=yyy'


async def test_bonsai():
    async with client.connect(is_async=True, timeout=2.0) as conn:
        res = await conn.search(dn, 2, filter)
        print(res)


loop = get_event_loop()

start_bonsai = time()
loop.run_until_complete(test_bonsai())
stop_bonsai = time() - start_bonsai


server = Server(host=host, port=port, use_ssl=False, get_info=ALL)


def test_ldap3():
    with Connection(server,
                    auto_bind=True,
                    client_strategy='ASYNC',
                    user=user,
                    password=pswd
                    ) as conn:

        id_mess = conn.search(search_base=dn,
                              search_filter='({})'.format(filter),
                              attributes=ALL_ATTRIBUTES,
                              )
        print(conn.get_response(id_mess))


start_ldap3 = time()
test_ldap3()
stop_ldap3 = time() - start_bonsai

print('ldap3:  ', stop_ldap3)
print('bonsai: ', stop_bonsai)
```

And here is the result itself:
```
ldap3:   0.4059  sec
bonsai:  0.0057 sec
```
What am I doing wrong? What is the difference? 

Thank you

issue_title: How to manually add features and relations?
issue_body:
Thanks again for `gffutils`. It's a lifesaver.

I'm detecting *de novo* exons based on junction reads and want to add them to a `gffutils.FeatureDB`with a specific ID (`exon:chrom:start-stop:strand`) but they're getting added with an auto-named id `exon_1` rather than the one I gave it.

[Here](https://github.com/YeoLab/outrigger/commit/d45338b97dc2fb96b7e8297e5ec5923a533e576c#diff-0573fcefb6ee0c7b63d9aec6e2ebf770R124) is the commit I'm talking about.

So, even though I create features this way:

        exon_id = 'exon:{chrom}:{start}-{stop}:'.format(
            chrom=chrom, start=start, stop=stop)
        de_novo_exons = [gffutils.Feature(chrom, source='outrigger_de_novo',
                                     featuretype='exon', start=start, end=stop,
                                     strand=g.strand, id=exon_id + g.strand,
                                     attributes=dict(g.attributes.items()))
                         for g in overlapping_genes]

When I print the exon id, I get `exon_1` instead of `exon:chr10:128491290-128491347:-`.

```
progress('\tFound a novel exon ({}) in the gene {} '
                     '({})'.format(exon.id, gene.id,
                                   ','.join(gene.attributes['gene_name'])))
```

```
2016-08-17 13:43:47	Detecting de novo exons based on gaps between junctions ...
2016-08-17 13:43:47	Found new exon between outrigger.Region (junction:chr10:128491034-128491289:-) and outrigger.Region (junction:chr10:128491348-128492058:-)
2016-08-17 13:43:47		Found novel exon at exon:chr10:128491290-128491347:
2016-08-17 13:43:47		Found a novel exon (exon_1) in the gene ENSMUSG00000090841.1 (Myl6)
```

Reading the documentation for `gffutils.Feature`, I see that the ID is ignored if it's not from a database ...

```
id : None or string
    Database-specific primary key for this feature.  The only time this
    should not be None is if this feature is coming from a database, in
    which case it will be filled in automatically.
```

So is the solution here to aggregate all the exons I want to add, create a separate database with all the novel exons, then do `db.update(de_novo_exon_db)` once it's all ready? That seems clunky. Is there a way to override and force usage of the feature ID provided in the construction of a `gffutils.Feature` object?

issue_title: Training simple_object_detector in Python and then loading it in C++ problem
issue_body:
I'm training a `simple_object_detector` using dlib's Python API:

```python
options = dlib.simple_object_detector_training_options()
options.add_left_right_image_flips = False
options.C = 5
options.num_threads = 3
options.be_verbose = True

dlib.train_simple_object_detector("data.xml", "detector.svm", options)
```

If I then load this detector in Python using `dlib.simple_object_detector("detector.svm")` and try to evaluate it on a dataset, it works really really well. The problem is that we need to use the detector in C++, and there it barely works.

The `detector.svm` is loaded like this in C++:

```c++
typedef scan_fhog_pyramid<pyramid_down<6> > image_scanner_type; 
image_scanner_type scanner;

object_detector<image_scanner_type> detector;
deserialize("face_detector.svm") >> detector;
```

It only works on very few of our test images (like 5 out of 100), whereas in Python we get most of them correctly (like 90-95 out of 100). I assume that the .svm file is not loaded correctly in C++.

If I load the .svm file in Python using `dlib.fhog_object_detector("detector.svm")`, then I actually get the same behaviour as in C++: It only works for the same 5 images. But this is a bit confusing, because the detector wasn't trained as an fhog object detector, but the method also doesn't throw an error.

There is another weird behaviour: The bounding box found in C++ seems to be different from the one found in Python. Here's the bounding box we get for one of the test images in Python:

![py-frame35](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1564556/27741996-51ea02b4-5db7-11e7-834b-df3fbb3918fb.png)

And here is the bounding box generated by the exact same .svm file in C++ (The screenshot is a bit bigger, but of course both programs run on the same image):

![cpp-frame35](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1564556/27741739-67820672-5db6-11e7-97dd-ccd320d5f49e.jpg)

It seems like in C++ the detector tries a much larger sliding window, and therefore doesn't find any objects if they are smaller.

This is the C++ code that's being used for giving the OpenCV image to the dlib detector:

```c++
cv::Mat imgForDlib = cv_ptr->image.clone();
dlib::cv_image<dblib::bgr_pixel> cimg(imgForDlib);
std::vector<dlib::rectangle> objects = detector(cimg);
```

Is that detector not loaded correctly in C++? Or is it a problem with the image datatype that we give to dlib?
We would greatly appreciate any help!

issue_title: Make curve-curve intersection check duplicates by (x, y) not (s, t)
issue_body:
This will hopefully eliminate the need for tweaking `SIMILAR_ULPS`.

In [`add_intersection()`][1] we check:

```python
for existing_s, existing_t in intersections:
    if (_helpers.ulps_away(
            existing_s, s, num_bits=_SIMILAR_ULPS) and
            _helpers.ulps_away(
                existing_t, t, num_bits=_SIMILAR_ULPS)):
        return
```

The idea is that by checking the potentially new `(s, t)` value against the existing ones, some roundoff issues can make it seem like the values are different. But if we check that `B1(s) == B1(s_new)` and `B2(t) == B2(t_new)` this should account for "negligible" differences in the parameters. (It's also worth noting that we "need" to have `B1(s_new) == B2(t_new)`.)

----

Similarly on the [Fortran side][2]:

```fortran
do index_ = 1, num_intersections
   if ( &
        ulps_away(intersections(1, index_), s, &
                 SIMILAR_ULPS, VECTOR_CLOSE_EPS) .AND. &
        ulps_away(intersections(2, index_), t, &
                  SIMILAR_ULPS, VECTOR_CLOSE_EPS)) then
      return
   end if
end do
```

[1]: https://github.com/dhermes/bezier/blob/5b1082f00dd1f5c77bc87b207591ff6e75a8ed25/src/bezier/_geometric_intersection.py#L696-L701
[2]: https://github.com/dhermes/bezier/blob/5b1082f00dd1f5c77bc87b207591ff6e75a8ed25/src/bezier/curve_intersection.f90#L477-L485

issue_title: General questions
issue_body:
I have a few general questions regarding bt that I would like to ask. I hope it is acceptable to ask questions like this here.

1) For what type of investment style was this project created? It seems to me that bt is focussed on longer-term portfolios with concentration on rebalancing. Would bt also work well for short-term investments? I am not referring to HFT, but daily trading or perhaps hourly trading.

2) Is it at all possible to use bt for live trading through extending the code? I assume it is not since it seems that bt is not event-driven. Could you provide some clarity?

3) The roadmap does not provide any specific features that could be implemented, but is a little vague. What are the current shortcomings of this library? What would be valuable features to add other than providing more algos?

I have scanned through the code and would just like to complement you on your neat and thorough coding style. Your use of comments is better than most of the code I see these days...

issue_title: suggested docs warning
issue_body:
A common way to use Statsd is to create a client for a given process and continually reuse it.  That is fine, except for an annoying implementation detail that I noticed while securing a box.

When data is first sent via `socket` ( https://github.com/jsocol/pystatsd/blob/master/statsd/client.py#L145-L151 ) Python appears to bind on all interfaces (e.g. `0.0.0.0`) to a random port in effort of accepting a response.  (sidenote: it took a while to pinpoint this onto pystatsd).  It would make sense to note this inherent behavior in the docs/readme for the next person who is securing their box.

issue_title: Infinite loop on ThreadPool
issue_body:
Hi Matteo,

I left for the end the most complex test case.

The code below may seem a bit long...

What we may notice is that I launch a ThreadPool with 10000 tasks in it.
At a certaint time, I raise an exception : either in the callback function, either directly in the function launched by the task, in order to test the pool exit.
The pool is then stopped doing so :
```python
_POOL.stop()
_POOL.join()
```
see `pool_stop`function.

Also, the behaviour is different, wether I have a waiting time in the called function, or not.

You should copy/paste the code below and run it as it is.
I hope, with these explanations, it will be OK !

Thank you very much again for your help and all my apologies for this poor  piece of code... (you won't believe it) !

Regards,

Philippe


```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

import sys
from datetime import date, datetime, timedelta
import time, random
import threading
from threading import current_thread
from pebble import ThreadPool
from pebble import ProcessPool

def init_pool():
    global _POOL_RUNNING

    print "%s - New created worker  - _POOL_RUNNING = %s" % (datetime.now(), _POOL_RUNNING)
    return

def pool_stop(cpt):
    global _POOL
    global _LOCK
    global _STOP

    print "Call fonction pool_stop - cpt = %s" % cpt
    with _LOCK:
        if not _STOP:
            _STOP = True
            #_POOL.close()
            print "Call _POOL.stop() ... : _POOL.active = %s" % _POOL.active
            _POOL.stop()
            print "Call _POOL.join() ... : _POOL.active = %s" % _POOL.active
            _POOL.join(0)
        else:
            print "_POOL has been stoped - cpt = %s" % cpt
    print "Exit function pool_stop - cpt = %s" % cpt
    return

def work_todo(cpt, a, b, mode = 'callback', max_hold_time = None, test_exit = False, log = False):
    global _STOP
    global _LOCK
    global _POOL

    res1 = None
    res2 = None
    wait_time = None
    if not _STOP:
        try:
            res1 = a + b
            res2 = a * b
            if max_hold_time:
                wait_time = random.randint(1, max_hold_time)
                print "%s - %s/%s - Wait %ss..." % (cpt, a, b, wait_time)
                time.sleep(wait_time)
                print "%s - %s/%s - End of wait." % (cpt, a, b)
            if not mode and log:
                print "{0}- {1}/{2} - {3} --> {4} secondes -- ({1} + {2}) = {5} ◊◊◊ ({1} * {2}) = {6}".format(
                    cpt, a, b, current_thread().ident, wait_time, res1, res2)

            if test_exit and (not mode or mode == 'err_caller') and b != 0 and b % random.randint(5, 10) == 0:
                # Throw exception to test pool exit :
                msg = "cpt = %s - exception created in work_done to test pool exit" % cpt
                raise RuntimeError(msg)
        except Exception as erreur:
            print "cpt = %s - ERROR IN work_todo >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> %s" % (cpt, erreur)
            pool_stop(cpt)
    else:
        print "cpt = %s - POOL has been stopped !" % cpt

    return mode, test_exit, max_hold_time, cpt, wait_time, log, a, b, res1, res2, current_thread().ident

def work_done(task):
    global _POOL
    global _LOCK
    global _STOP

    cpt = None
    try:
        mode, test_exit, max_hold_time, cpt, wait_time, log, a, b, res1, res2, thread_id = task.result()
        if log:
            print "DONE -> {0} - {1} - {2}/{3} - {4} --> {5} secondes -- POOL : {6}/{7} -- ({2} + {3}) = {8} ◊◊◊ ({2} * {3}) = {9}".format(
                datetime.now(), cpt, a, b, thread_id, wait_time, _POOL, _POOL.active, res1, res2)
        if mode == 'callback':
            if test_exit and b != 0 and b % random.randint(5, 10) == 0:
                msg = "cpt = %s - exception created in work_done to test pool exit" % cpt
                raise RuntimeError(msg)
    except Exception as erreur:
        print "cpt = %s - ERROR IN work_done >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> %s" % (cpt, erreur)
        pool_stop(cpt)
    return

def test_thread_pool(maxi, mode, max_hold_time, test_exit=False):
    global _STOP
    global _POOL
    global _POOL_RUNNING

    _STOP = False
    trace_fct =  "test_thread_pool(maxi=%s, mode=\"%s\", max_hold_time=%s, test_exit=%s) : %s tasks ..." % (maxi, mode, max_hold_time, test_exit, maxi*maxi)
    print "\n%s - BEGIN %s" % (datetime.now(), trace_fct)
    task = []
    try:
        _POOL_RUNNING = False
        with ThreadPool(max_workers = 5, max_tasks = 5, initializer = init_pool) as _POOL:
            cpt = 0
            for i in range(0, maxi):
                for j in range(0, maxi):
                    cpt += 1
                    if _STOP:
                        print "In test_thread_pool : cpt = %s - stop loading of POOL : POOL has been stopped !" % cpt
                        break

                    tache = _POOL.schedule(work_todo, args = (cpt, i, j, mode, max_hold_time, test_exit, False))
                    if mode in ['callback', 'err_caller']:
                        tache.add_done_callback(work_done)
                    task.append(tache)
                if _STOP:
                    break
            print "%s - Pool LOADED !!!!" % datetime.now()
            _POOL_RUNNING = True
    except Exception as erreur:
        print " ERROR IN test_thread_pool >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> ", erreur if erreur.message else type(erreur)
    print "\n%s - END %s" % (datetime.now(), trace_fct)
    return

# GLOBALS :
_STOP = False
_LOCK = threading.Semaphore()
_POOL = None
_POOL_RUNNING = False

# ------------- #
# Fonction main #
# ------------- #
def main(argv):

    maxi = 10
    # OK :
    #test_thread_pool(maxi=5, mode='callback', max_hold_time=0, test_exit=True)
    # NOK :
    test_thread_pool(maxi=100, mode='callback', max_hold_time=0, test_exit=True)
    # OK :
    #test_thread_pool(maxi=100, mode='callback', max_hold_time=5, test_exit=True)
    #test_thread_pool(maxi=10, mode='err_caller', max_hold_time=0, test_exit=True)
    #test_thread_pool(maxi=10, mode='', max_hold_time=0, test_exit=True)

    return

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main(sys.argv[1:])

```

issue_title: if path is empty, set the path to /
setting up multiple connections at the same time crashes
issue_body:
setting up multiple connections at the same time results in an exception 'Failed to connect to..'

tested with bluepy==1.1.4

Reproduce by executing:

```python
import threading
import time

from bluepy.btle import Peripheral, Scanner

macs = ["a0:e6:f8:ce:1a:02", "54:6c:0e:a2:de:87"]
peripherals = [Peripheral() for _ in xrange(len(macs))]


def do_connect(index):
    """ connecting thread """
    p = peripherals[index]
    mac = macs[index]

    print 'setup connection to %s' % mac

    p.connect(mac)
    time.sleep(2)
    p.disconnect()
    # extra sleep to make sure connection is closed
    time.sleep(3)

print 'try connecting sequentially'
thread0 = threading.Thread(target=do_connect, args=(0,))
thread0.setDaemon(True)

thread1 = threading.Thread(target=do_connect, args=(1,))
thread1.setDaemon(True)

thread0.start()
thread0.join()
thread1.start()
thread1.join()

print 'try in parallel'
thread0 = threading.Thread(target=do_connect, args=(0,))
thread0.setDaemon(True)

thread1 = threading.Thread(target=do_connect, args=(1,))
thread1.setDaemon(True)

thread0.start()
thread1.start()

thread0.join()
thread1.join()

print 'the end'

```

Keep in mind that the extra sleep is needed after the disconnect, otherwise the same connection could be used, and the test would pass.

I have an IGD that expects the AppPortMapping arguments to be in the same order as listed in argsdef_in. However, upnpclient can mix them up. As a result, my IGD returns error 402 (invalid args) and an HTTP Status of 500.

issue_title: strptime, strftime support
issue_body:
Hi, I understand you added this module as a way to make datetime parsing faster but with the caveat that we only deal with one input format. I was looking for a fast module that does the same thing as datetime to convert from multiple string formats.. unfortunately, I cannot control the input formats in this case. 

I stumbled upon Arrow but noticed its very slow. Since this is not a straight drop in from datetime would you be able to point me in the right direction to speedup ? Thanks

PS. Here is what I want to achieve, sadly I cannot with udatetime

```
STR_KNOWN_DATE_FORMATS = ['%Y-%m-%d', '%m/%d/%Y', '%m/%d/%y', '%y/%m/%d']

STR_KNOWN_TIME_FORMATS = ['%I:%M:%S %p', '%H:%M:%S', '%I:%M %p', '%H:%M %p',
                          '%I:%M%p', '%H:%M',
                          '%I:%M:%S%p']

# Known date and time formats, all combos
STR_KNOWN_DATETIME_FORMATS = []
for kd in STR_KNOWN_DATE_FORMATS:
    for kt in STR_KNOWN_TIME_FORMATS:
        STR_KNOWN_DATETIME_FORMATS.append('{} {}'.format(kd, kt))

STR_DATETIME_FORMAT = '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'
def date_and_time(given_date, given_time):
    """
    Normalize date and time values
    :param given_date: str, iso date i.e. 2016-03-01 (YYYY-MM-DD)
    :param given_time: str, iso time or None i.e 23:33:44 (HH:mm:ss)
    :return: str, date and time (YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss)
    """
    # Remove spaces
    given_time = given_time.strip()
    given_date = given_date.strip()

    # Join the date and time
    t_date = given_date + ' ' + given_time

    event_date = None
    for datetime_fmt in STR_KNOWN_DATETIME_FORMATS:
        try:
            event_date = datetime.datetime.strptime(t_date, datetime_fmt).strftime(STR_DATETIME_FORMAT)
            break
        except ValueError:
            continue
        except Exception as e:  # pragma: no cover
            logging.exception("date_and_time unhandled exception for date {}, format {}. {}"
                              "".format(t_date, datetime_fmt, e))
            continue

    # Unable to find the format given, we may need to add a new format
    if not event_date:
        logging.error("date_and_time unable to parse date '{}', time '{}'".format(given_date, given_time))

    return event_date
```

issue_title: Inaccurate reporting of process creation time via create_time() under Linux
issue_body:
It looks like the value reported in psutil via `create_time()` is inaccurate under Linux. It's too far in the past (by less than a second, in my tests). It looks like the offset is constant across various runs. I only tested this on Linux (both debian and arch). The issue appears in both python2 and python3.

I wrote a sample script to illustrate the problem. You'll notice that the reported `create_time()` of the forked process is too far back in the past: older than the current timestamp at the beginning of the script. For comparison, I also printed out the timestamp for the folder creation via `stat` in `/proc/<pid>` (well, modification time, I couldn't get the creation time for some reason).

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import subprocess
import time
import datetime

import psutil

PROCESS_TO_FORK = "python3"


def main():
    initial_time = datetime.datetime.now()
    print("time now: {}".format(initial_time))
    p = subprocess.Popen(
        PROCESS_TO_FORK,
        stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
        stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL)
    proc = psutil.Process(p.pid)
    stat_out = subprocess.check_output(
        ['stat', '-c', '%y', '/proc/{}'.format(p.pid)])

    ct = datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(proc.create_time())
    print("create_time from psutil: {}".format(ct))
    print("create_time from stat: {}".format(stat_out.decode("utf-8")))
    print("process uptime according to psutil: {}".format(
        time.time() - proc.create_time()))
    # print("process uptime according to psutil: {}".format(
    #     datetime.datetime.now() - ct))

    end_time = datetime.datetime.now()
    print("time now: {}".format(end_time))
    print("script runtime: {}s".format(
        (end_time - initial_time).total_seconds()))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

A sample output would be:
```
$ time ./test_create.py
time now: 2016-08-29 14:38:52.471206
create_time from psutil: 2016-08-29 14:38:51.930000
create_time from stat: 2016-08-29 14:38:52.469848115 +0200

process uptime according to psutil: 0.5433444976806641
time now: 2016-08-29 14:38:52.473368
script runtime: 0.002162s
./test_create.py  0.07s user 0.00s system 97% cpu 0.072 total
```

As can be seen from the output, the process runtime is larger than the script runtime, which is impossible. Note that the time obtained from `stat` is also a bit in the past compared to the initial timestamp, so that's also wrong, but it's not off by as much. The fact that the resolution of create_time() seems to be many digits is misleading if its accuracy is actually within 1s.

Other tools seem to only show process creation-time/runtime up to a second accuracy. Is it impossible to get a more fine grained value? It seems unlikely because the offset/error is [almost] constant.

Reference: http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/7870/how-to-check-how-long-a-process-has-been-running

issue_title: dict object has no attribute iteritems
issue_body:
Hello,

Here is a program I quickly put together in order to play around with Deco:

```py
import collections
import time
from deco import concurrent, synchronized

@concurrent
def compute(x, y):
    print("Compute %s + %s ..." % (x, y))
    time.sleep(2)
    return x + y

@synchronized
def print_sum():
    results = collections.defaultdict(dict)
    results[1] = compute(1, 2)
    results[2] = compute(1, 2)
    results[3] = compute(1, 2)
    results[4] = compute(1, 2)
    final_result = results[1] + results[2] + results[3] + results[4]
    print("combined results {}".format(final_result))

print_sum()
```

Am I mistaken, or missing something obvious?

issue_title: Add support for different read/write schemas
issue_body:
A pain point that often exists with automatic swagger specs with DRF is they reuse the same definition for retrieve/list/post endpoints. This leads to painful client side SDKs generated with `swagger-codegen` as most/all fields on the resulting model will be optional, requiring SDK consumers to litter their code with null checks (or ignore them at their peril).

A motivating example of this problem is the `id` field that most django models will have. This field is `required` on reads (the model will always have an id when retrieving/listing) but a `read_only` field for creates (not required for post requests). Another example would be a field that is derrives from a `null=False` model field (required at the DB level) but is not required at the API level (perhaps it's generated by the server's business logic).

Can (does) `drf-yasg`'s inspection of serializers find these characteristics and if so, can it be shown in the resulting spec by defining multiple definitions for a serializer and referencing them appropriately in spec paths?

For context, our team generates SDKs for our APIs in many type safe languages and model definitions such as:

```yaml
MyModel:
    type: object
    required:
        - name
    properties:
        id:
            type: number
        name:
            type: string
```

are a headache to use because the resulting model will label `id` as optional, meaning it must be checked before being passed to other SDK methods that expect a value. E.g.

```typescript
interface MyModel {
    id?: number;
    name: string;
}

// ...

api.updateMyModel({ id: instance.id }) // compilation error because `.id` might be undefined
```

issue_title: `datetime` object returned should not be naive
issue_body:
I have something like this in my OFX file: `<DTSERVER>20150804111745[-3:GMT]`. When parsing it using `ofxtools` I get a naive `datetime.datetime` object:

```python
>>> from ofxtools import OFXTree
>>> tree = OFXTree()
>>> tree.parse('test.ofx')
>>> converted = tree.convert()
>>> converted.sonrs.dtserver
datetime.datetime(2015, 8, 4, 14, 17, 45)
```

The returned object is correct (`2015-08-04 11:17:45 GMT-3` is `2015-08-04 14:17:45 UTC`) but it lost the timezone information after conversion.

I think the object returned should be something like this:

```python
import pytz  # pip install pytz

offset = '-3'  # take it from OFX
timezone = pytz.FixedOffset(int(offset) * 60)
object_returned = datetime.datetime(2015, 8, 4, 11, 17, 45, tzinfo=timezone)
```

issue_title: KafkaConsumer pattern on newly created topic
issue_body:
Doing this works well on existing topics (let say 'crawl.1.fetched' and 'crawl.2.fetched'):
```
self.consumer = KafkaConsumer(bootstrap_servers="kafka:9092", group_id=self.group_id, auto_offset_reset='earliest')
self.consumer.subscribe(pattern="crawl.*.fetched")
```
if I create a third one 'crawl.3.fetch' and I am expecting the KafkaConsumer to start processing this new topic but it doesn't. If I restart the application of course "crawl.3.fetch" get processed.

If I use a topic pattern with the kafka console consumer tool, it is working as expected
```
/opt/kafka/bin/kafka-console-consumer.sh --zookeeper zookeeper:2181 --topic crawl.*.fetched--from-beginning
```
This consumer will process any new topic created with the pattern crawl.*.fetched.

Is there a way to have this behavior with kafka-python?

issue_title: Help: Keep getting Fetch offset X is out of range. Resetting offset for topic-partition...
issue_body:
Hey,

Suddenly I started getting the following "info" messages every 10 minutes:
```
fetcher->_handle_fetch_response():750 - Fetch offset 17250 is out of range for topic-partition TopicPartition(topic=u'<TOPIC_NAME>', partition=0)
fetcher->_handle_fetch_response():753 - Resetting offset for topic-partition TopicPartition(topic=u'<TOPIC_NAME>', partition=0)
```
Also, the highwater seems to stay 0 (meaning that committing offsets fail?), Assignment dump:
```
[{'topic': u'<TOPIC_NAME>', 'highwater': 0, 'partition': 2, 'lag': 0, 'committed': 0}, {'topic': u'<TOPIC_NAME>', 'highwater': 0, 'partition': 0, 'lag': 0, 'committed': 0}, {'topic': u'<TOPIC_NAME>', 'highwater': 0, 'partition': 1, 'lag': 0, 'committed': 0}]
```

Notes:
1. I have 3 brokers.
2. Currently, only partition 0 should contain data, all the other partitions are empty.
3. I have 1 consumer fetching from the 3 brokers using HighLevelConsumer.
4. I have 2 other topics that are working fine without this behavior (on the same cluster).
5. I didn't deploy any new code or changed the infrastructure, it just started happening, all the usual metrics of kafka are healthy and there are no apparent errors.

Consequences: 
With auto_offset_reset set to 'earliest'/'latest' (doesn't matter since the offset stays 0), every 10 minutes, all of the messages in the topic (which are still retained - 7 days in my case for the retention period) are being re-fetched every 10 minutes!

Thoughts:
1. Maybe something is wrong with Zookeeper and it doesn't store offsets for this topic properly?
2. Maybe for some reason I'm not really committing? How can I check that?

Thanks,
Ilai.

issue_title: Licensing clarification
issue_body:
sslyze lists it's license as GPLv2. Can you clarify if this is an intentional choice, or one made for historical reasons? 

GPLv2 makes perfect sense for the use of sslyze as a standalone tool, and preventing users from building a proprietary SSL analysis tool based off that codebase. 

However, I've got a use case that would involved deploying sslyze as part of a Software-as-a-Service platform. Under the terms of the GPLv2, this would be a completely legitimate usage of sslyze, and I would *not* be required to distribute the source code for my SaaS platform (see [this FAQ on the GNU website](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-faq.html#UnreleasedMods)).

While I'm completely covered under the *letter* of the law, I'd rather not violate the *spirit* of the law in the way the project intended the sslyze API and codebase to be used. 

Are you able to:
* Clarify that you have no objection to sslyze being used as part of a proprietary SaaS deployment; and/or
* Provide (either to me specifically, or more generally) a license with more liberal terms (LGPL would be fine; MIT/BSD would be even better); or
* Modify the license to be AGPL, or something that covers the SaaS use case?

issue_title: Exposing other row and cell attributes of tables
issue_body:
Currently the parser for table data ([`Table.getdata`](https://github.com/5j9/wikitextparser/blob/6b91b1cc7033a605ff0c5bbc0523f0d4c49f09e9/wikitextparser/table.py#L165)) only returns the cells' content, but ignores all row attributes except `rowspan` and all cell attributes except `colspan`. 

Both [`NEWLINE_CELL_REGEX`](https://github.com/5j9/wikitextparser/blob/6b91b1cc7033a605ff0c5bbc0523f0d4c49f09e9/wikitextparser/table.py#L21) and [`INLINE_HAEDER_CELL_REGEX`](
https://github.com/5j9/wikitextparser/blob/6b91b1cc7033a605ff0c5bbc0523f0d4c49f09e9/wikitextparser/table.py#L52) already contain named groups for all attributes. Would it be possible to expose these in `getdata` (or possibly another method)? I was thinking the format could be tuples `[(row_attributes, row_data), ...]`, where `row_data` is `[(cell_attributes, cell_data), ...]` (instead of the current `[[cell_data], ...]`.

_From [Dimitri....@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/108225486573607287600/) on June 25, 2014 10:25:32_

I am attempting to read DICOM files I have received from another lab. They were initially produced by a Siemens TrioTim MRI scanner running syngo MR B17.

An InvalidDicomError exception is raised whenever I attempt to read *any* of these files. Using "force=True" does fix the problem: dicom.read_file() succeeds and I can read tags such as SeriesDescription.

These DICOM files indeed miss a DICOM header and specifically the prefix "DICM" at position 0x80:

0000000: 0800 0500 0a00 0000 4953 4f5f 4952 2031  ........ISO_IR 1

issue_title: Async decorators
issue_body:
How about adding decorators for async procedures ? 

Something like : 

```

def require_async(description, predicate):
    def asyncdec(func):
        @wraps(func)
        @dpcontracts.require(description, predicate)
        async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            return await func(*args, **kwargs)
        return wrapper
    return asyncdec
```

or maybe better : 
```

import dpcontracts
from functools import wraps

def require_async(description, predicate):
    requirement = dpcontracts.require(description, predicate)
    def asyncdec(func):
        funcreq = requirement(func)
        @wraps(func)
        async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            funcreq(args)
            return await func(*args, **kwargs)
        return wrapper
    return asyncdec

```

Could then be used like that : 
```
    import trio

    @require_async("n is an int", lambda args: isinstance(args.n, int))
    async def return_delay(n: int) -> int:
        await trio.sleep(2)
        return n

    res = trio.run(return_delay, "bob")

    print(res)

```

issue_title: move_right(n) is not possible // cuf1 vs cuf
issue_body:
While documenting internals, noticed:

``move_right`` is sugar for ``cuf1``. This means ``term.move_right`` can move the cursor right one cell (non-destructively).

However, there is no sugar for ``cuf``.
This means ``term.move_right(20) == term.move_right()``:

```
In [14]: term.move_right(20)
Out[14]: u'\x1b[C'

In [15]: term.move_right
Out[15]: u'\x1b[C'
```

I'd like to think term.move_right(n) and move_left can intelligently chose cuf/cub when n is specified and greater than 1. When 1, cuf1/cub1 should be used.  Otherwise, ``''`` for 0, or negative values.

issue_title: Error with Unicode/UTF-8 Titles
issue_body:
While retrieving the title of a TV show, I got the following error:
```bash
'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\u2026' in position 16: ordinal not in range(128)
```
As it turned out, the reason for the error was the title of »Impastor« 1x02, which has a beautiful one-letter ellipsis [unicode character](http://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/2026/index.htm) included on trakt (»[On the Third Day…](http://trakt.tv/shows/impastor/seasons/1/episodes/2)«). Unfortunately, PyTrakt does not seem to handle it properly.

Here is a minimal example:
```python
from trakt import tv
print tv.TVEpisode("impastor", 1, 2)
```

For me, I fixed it in line [L677 of tv.py](https://github.com/moogar0880/PyTrakt/blob/master/trakt/tv.py#L677), where I replaced `self.title` with `self.title.encode('utf-8')`. It seems kind of hack-ish, hence I did not open a pull request. For my taste, you probably want to built in the UTF-8 conversion earlier in the API retrieval process, as, apparently, trakt seems to have UTF-8 characters now.

issue_title: mlaunch failing to connect to a standalone SSL mongod
issue_body:
I tried launching a cluster with this command: 
  mlaunch --single --dir $dir --name ruby-driver-rs \
    --wiredTigerCacheSizeGB 1 --setParameter enableTestCommands=1 --port 27400 \
    --sslMode requireSSL --sslPEMKeyFile `pwd`/cert/127.0.0.1.pem \
    --binarypath /usr/bin
It has --sslMode and --sslPEMKeyFile set. The cluster was launched, however mtools was sitting (hanging) for a long time with no output. Adding --verbose produced: 
waiting for nodes to start...
Examining mlaunch source, it looks for SSL args in: 
        for parser in self.ssl_args, self.ssl_client_args:
The two arguments I passed in are in neither of these option groups, hence it seems that the mongo client was created without SSL being set and was trying to connect to my SSL mongod via a non-SSL connection, which then wouldn't work. The following quick hack fixes this issue: https://github.com/rueckstiess/mtools/compare/develop...p-mongo:ssl-single-fix?expand=1 I'm sure there is a better way to accomplish this though. ### Expected behavior mlaunch should be able to launch a standalone SSL mongod with a self-signed certificate successfully. ### Actual/current behavior mlaunch does launch the mongod but hangs trying to connect to it. ### Steps to reproduce the actual/current behavior 
  mlaunch --single --dir $dir --name ruby-driver-rs \
    --wiredTigerCacheSizeGB 1 --setParameter enableTestCommands=1 --port 27400 \
    --sslMode requireSSL --sslPEMKeyFile `pwd`/cert/127.0.0.1.pem \
    --binarypath /usr/bin
### Environment | Software | Version | ---------------- | ------- | mtools | develop | MongoDB server | 3.6.5 | Operating system | Debian testing

issue_title: refactor code for class IDF and class EpBunch
issue_body:
The child classes that lead to IDF and EpBunch reflect the chronological order in which the classes were developed. This makes it hard for someone reading them to make sense of it
There are two options in refactoring them. 
1. Make a single class that embodies the entire functionality OR
2. Retain the child classes, but have a logical structure within the class inheritance

Earlier discussion on this issue happens here 
https://github.com/santoshphilip/eppy/issues/62#issuecomment-178088044
and
https://github.com/santoshphilip/eppy/issues/62#issuecomment-183790728

issue_title: pretty_midi.PrettyMIDI.key_signature_changes, time_signature_changes, and lyrics are not listed in the docs
Workflow when updating CEF version
issue_body:
1. Update src/include/ headers with CEF repo's include/ directory
  - ignore the capi/ directory
  - keep cefpython's modifications in cef_path_util.h done by Issue #231 patch (see patches/)
  - keep cefpython's modifications in cef_drag_data.h done by Issue #251 patch (see patches/)
  - if API changed in CEF then additional modifications may be required in cefpython's code

2. Update the patches/ directory. When a file that is being modified by the patch was modified in upstream, CEF patcher tool will always fail to apply the patch, even though there are no conflicts. The solution is to regenerate patches. View which patches need to be regenerated in patches/patch.py and for each do these steps:
  - Copy the patch to appropriate CEF or Chromium directory and apply the patch with `git apply -p0 issueXXX.patch` command
  - Delete the patch file and generate a new patch file with `git diff --no-prefix --relative > issueXXX.patch` command
  - Update the patch in the patches/ directory

3. Enter the build directory and run the automate.py tool:

  ```
  cd cefpython/build/
  python ../tools/automate.py --build-cef --ninja-jobs 6 --cef-branch 2883 --force-chromium-update
  ```
  - The `--force-chromium-update` flag needs to be specified when Chromium version changed since last update. When just rebuilding project files you don't need to specify it, it will slow down the process.
  - If the `--cef-branch` flag isn't specified then CEF branch is taken from the src/version/cef_version_xxx.h file

4. Make sure that patches were applied successfully. Currently when patch fails, CEF will continue building. You need to go through output and find messages about patches being applied, like below. In this case issue125 patch failed. Patches issue231 and issue251 succeeded. If you have problems finding these messages, after you've built new CEF, try running the automate.py script again and it should appear somewhere at the beginning.

  ```
  ...
  --> already patched  ../chrome/app/generated_resources.grd
  --> already patched  ../components/plugins/renderer/loadable_plugin_placeholder.cc
  --> already patched  ../components/plugins/renderer/webview_plugin.cc
  --> already patched  ../components/plugins/renderer/webview_plugin.h
  Reading patch file /home/cz/github/cefpython/build/chromium/src/cef/patch/patches/issue125.patch
  --> file is not patched - failed hunk: 1
  --> source file is different - ../net/http/http_cache_transaction.cc
  Reading patch file /home/cz/github/cefpython/build/chromium/src/cef/patch/patches/issue231.patch
  --> successfully patched ./include/capi/cef_path_util_capi.h
  --> successfully patched ./include/cef_path_util.h
  --> successfully patched ./libcef/browser/path_util_impl.cc
  --> successfully patched ./libcef_dll/libcef_dll.cc
  --> successfully patched ./libcef_dll/wrapper/libcef_dll_wrapper.cc
  Reading patch file /home/cz/github/cefpython/build/chromium/src/cef/patch/patches/issue251.patch
  --> successfully patched ./include/capi/cef_drag_data_capi.h
  --> successfully patched ./include/cef_drag_data.h
  --> successfully patched ./libcef/browser/osr/web_contents_view_osr.cc
  --> successfully patched ./libcef/common/drag_data_impl.cc
  --> successfully patched ./libcef/common/drag_data_impl.h
  --> successfully patched ./libcef_dll/cpptoc/drag_data_cpptoc.cc
  --> successfully patched ./libcef_dll/ctocpp/drag_data_ctocpp.cc
  --> successfully patched ./libcef_dll/ctocpp/drag_data_ctocpp.h
  ```

5. Update the "src/version/cef_version_xxx.h" file (xxx is platform) with the contents of the "build/chromium/src/cef/binary_distrib/cef_binary_xxx/include/cef_version.h" file


NOTES:
- There must be a way to automate regenerating patches. Take a look at upstream patch tools or ask on the CEF Forum. 
- Get rid of Issue #231 and #251 patches by integrating it in upstream. Patches already sent, patch 231 needs to be reworked, patch 251 is still waiting for review. Pull requests created in upstream:
  - https://bitbucket.org/chromiumembedded/cef/pull-requests/66/override-paths-dir_exe-dir_module-on-linux/diff
  - https://bitbucket.org/chromiumembedded/cef/pull-requests/80/add-functions-to-support-image/diff
- Upstream [Issue #2012](https://bitbucket.org/chromiumembedded/cef/issues/2012/enhance-automate-gitpy-error-handling-when) is to enhance automate-git tool so that it stops when patching fails

There are a number of problems with the example code in the

issue_title: Verify encoding awareness and unicode handling
issue_body:
We need better test coverage of Unicode functionality.  This ties into Python3 compatibility, because otherwise that enhancement has high potential to break things. 

Components:

* Verify that request bodies specified in YAML can pass Unicode or binary data through
* Verify that headers get encoding of unicode handled correctly (this should be escaped appropriately)
* Verify that URLs correctly escape unicode and special characters via URL encoding
* Verify that when read from file, unicode awareness is preserved
* Internally insure that data has some internal encoding awareness (probably prefer storing raw bytes for request/response bodies). 

Nice to have:

* Unicode-friendly use in templating, not just byte-string

issue_title: Checking for unnecessary flake8-ignore entries
issue_body:
It would be nice if there was a way to detect unnecessary flake8-ignore entries, i.e., which are not actually suppressing any error.  The rationale is that some projects will start by adding a lot of entries to flake8-ignore (e.g. because they have a lot of older code which does not follow PEP8, and they mostly want the new code to follow PEP8), but sometimes some files will get cleaned, making some flake8-ignore entries obsolete.  If the entries remain, nothing prevents the code from becoming "PEP8-dirty" again, so it would be preferable to keep flake8-ignore up to date instead.

Glob patterns and ALL may be trickier to get right, but even a version where they are not handled would be appreciated.

I guess a way to implement this would be to call flake8 with no ignore flags, do the filtering yourself in a post-processing step, and notify (if requested) of the unnecessary ignores.

issue_title: remote tempdir only works on Linux-like systems
issue_body:
`BaseRemoteMachine.tempdir` runs `mktemp -d`, with no other parameters.

With the Todd Miller or GNU coreutils versions of `mktemp`, this works fine (at least under GNU, it uses a template of `tmp.XXXXXXXXXX`).

But with most other implementations, it's an error. The traditional command (it's not actually standardized, but was added in OpenBSD and FreeBSD and copied from there to many other systems) requires a template parameter. Most current *BSD versions allow a prefix instead of a template, but you still have to specify one or the other.

This means that if you try to use `tempdir` on a `RemoteMachine` that's connected to, say, OS X, or FreeBSD, you get a `ProcessExecutionError`.

This also means that RPyC ZeroDeploy doesn't work on OS X, FreeBSD, etc. targets.

Changing the command to explicitly use `tmp.XXXXXXXXXX` will make it work _almost_ everywhere, and work exactly the same as it has on almost every linux distro (and on Cygwin), which I think is the best option.

issue_title: Statistical distribution tests have a massive case of multiple testing
issue_body:
This isn't a correctness problem because it increases the set of false positive failures. It's annoying because it makes the build flaky though!

Proposed plan of action:

   * Make individual tests run at a much stricter significance level. e.g. divide it by the number of tests.
   * Use the Benjamini–Hochberg–Yekutieli procedure to do a statistical test on the p values for all the tests to see if any of them are significant with a 1% false positive rate.

    return serializer.deserialize(serialized)

issue_title: Coveralls isn't recognising information in uploads
issue_body:
Hi, i've started using this great proyect some weeks ago. It's a great proyect very easy to use!.

Since last sunday (31/5), coveralls stoped showing me last code coverage updates. All uploads are marked as if there are no valid files ([build 77](https://coveralls.io/builds/2692982), [build 78](https://coveralls.io/builds/2693648), [build 79](https://coveralls.io/builds/2694436), [build 80](https://coveralls.io/builds/2695006), [build 81](https://coveralls.io/builds/2695243), [build 82](https://coveralls.io/builds/2695466), [build 83](https://coveralls.io/builds/2695527) ). I've not change `cpp-coveralls` command line configuration, so i really don't know if it's coveralls fault, mine o other thing.

I'm uploading data using [this `.travis.yml`](https://github.com/NickCis/7552-taller-prog-2-2015-C1/blob/master/.travis.yml), bassically the line executed is this:

    coveralls -i src -i test -e src/coverage.cpp -E '.*\/mongoose\/mongoose\.[hc]' -E '.*CMakeFiles/feature_tests\..*' -E '.*CMakeCXXCompilerId\.cpp' -E '.*CMakeCCompilerId\.c' -E '.*\/test\/.*\.(h|hpp|cpp)$' -r ./

The proyect is usign `CMake`, under the folder `build` the main program is built and in the folder `test` test binaries are built. All the binaries are compiled with gcov flags and executed.

Lastest build is [this one](https://travis-ci.org/NickCis/7552-taller-prog-2-2015-C1/builds/64865389) and the last line of the output of `cpp-coveralls` is this:

    {u'url': u'https://coveralls.io/jobs/6323819', u'message': u'Job #82.1'}

If i execute the command in my machine and dump `cpp-coveralls` to a json i get [this output](https://gist.github.com/NickCis/c04333f6947bc1e00de6). And the last line of `cpp-coveralls` is this one:

    {u'url': u'https://coveralls.io/jobs/6323949', u'message': u'Job #83.1'}

As far as i understand all the files are in that json. If i upload the result locally i get the same problem with no valid files.

The coverall page of the proyect is: https://coveralls.io/r/NickCis/7552-taller-prog-2-2015-C1

Thanks in advance!

                                     timeout=self.timeout)
```

Watcher is a auto-start daemon thread.

```
class Watcher(threading.Thread):

    def __init__(self, watchstub, timeout=None):
        threading.Thread.__init__(self)
        self.timeout = timeout
        self._watch_id_callbacks = {}
        self._watch_id_queue = queue.Queue()
        self._watch_id_lock = threading.Lock()
        self._watch_requests_queue = queue.Queue()
        self._watch_response_iterator = \
            watchstub.Watch(self._requests_iterator)
        self._callback = None
        self.daemon = True
        self.start()
```

Is there any consideration about lazy watcher thread start?

issue_title: Feature implementation: Deep request serializer & different serializers for specific response codes
issue_body:
* DRF OpenAPI version: based on 1.0.0
* Python version: *
* Operating System: *

### Description

I extended the drf_openapi Django app to support detailed request serializers for requests. Then I also needed different serializers for different status codes (e.g. 200 has a specific response and some error, maybe 503 has a completely different one).
This is already done, but I am not sure if this is compatible to the OpenAPI spec.
If you are interested in this feature, then I would fork this and create a pull request.

### What I Did

Implemented the above functionality.
The drf_openapi module searches automatically for the correct serializer depending on the response code of the views' method.
Here are some screenshots of the extended usage:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9704553/31680497-c6d10434-b374-11e7-97e4-17deb4f49c7f.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9704553/31680544-e14f8434-b374-11e7-974b-36d6670d88d5.png)

Here is the django code for defining the views' serializers:
```
@view_config(request_serializer=UserLoginSerializer,
                 response_serializer={
                     200: (UserLoginResponseSerializer, 'Success'),
                     423: (UserLoginFailedResponseSerializer, 'Device not yet verified'),
                     400: (NoValidation, 'Bad Request - Invalid data given')
                 },
                 validate_response=True)
    def create(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        The user wants to authenticate against the backend and receives a token and his/her user ID on success.
        """
        ..... the views' code .....
        .....
        if something_went_wrong:
            return Response({'message': _('Verify device - email sent'), 'activation_id': device_activation_pin.pk},
                            status=status.HTTP_423_LOCKED)
        .....
        return Response({'message': _('logged in'), 'token': token.key, 'user_id': user.pk,
                         'created': token.created, 'device_id': device.pk},
                        status=status.HTTP_200_OK)
```
The "NoValidation" class passed as a serializer deactivates response validation for the specific response code.

issue_title: Does not support numeric abbreviation
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
`parser.parse` cannot properly parse numeric abbreviations
```python
>>> parser.parse("1k miles")
[Quantity(1, "unk mile")]
>>> parser.parse("1K miles")
[Quantity(1, "kelvin mile")]
>>> parser.parse("1M miles")
[Quantity(1, "metre mile")]
```

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Should be able to parse number abbreviations properly:

> In English numeric abbreviations and currency:
>     K for thousand (from kilo)
>     M for million
>     B for billion
>     T for trillion
> 
> In metric prefixes for SI measurements:
>     k for thousand (note lowercase)
>     M for million (from mega)
>     G for billion (from giga)
>     T for trillion (from tera)

Reference: https://english.stackexchange.com/a/112250

issue_title: Feature request: Shuffle support for playlists
issue_body:
It would be good to be able to play a shuffled playlist. 

Today I'm using the server playlist method to get access to the playlist. Then I pass that playlist off to the client playMedia method.

```
playlist = plex.playlist("Jazz Playlist")
client.playMedia(playlist)
```

I have no way to specify that I would like that playlist to be shuffled.

Inside the client playMedia() method a PlayQueue is created using createPlayQueue. The PlayQueue.create() method supports an option for setting shuffle=1. But there is not way to pass the option to the create() method. 

current...
```
    def playMedia(self, media, **params):
        if not self.server:
            raise Unsupported('A server must be specified before using this command.')
        server_url = media.server.baseurl.split(':')
        playqueue = self.server.createPlayQueue(media)
        self.sendCommand('playback/playMedia', **dict({
            'machineIdentifier': self.server.machineIdentifier,
            'address': server_url[1].strip('/'),
            'port': server_url[-1],
            'key': media.key,
            'containerKey': '/playQueues/%s?window=100&own=1' % playqueue.playQueueID,
        }, **params))
```

I could use the PlayQueue class to create a PlayQueue instance directly.

But, I can't pass playlist to the client playMedia() method.

The basic feature request is to be able to shuffle, repeat, etc. a playlist when played on a client.

The broader request might be to decouple the PlayQueue from the playMedia method. Or offer a new playMediaQueue (or some reasonable name) method.

Please let me know if I'm missing something and there is already a way to do this.

issue_title: Why does print() have an impact on ainput()?
issue_body:
I have the following simple test program to test ainput() behavior:

```python
import aioconsole
import asyncio

async def do_sleep(delay):
    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(delay)
        print('Did sleep {:.2f} seconds.'.format(delay))

async def print_input(prompt):
    while True:
        s = await aioconsole.ainput(prompt)
        print('Input was:', s)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.create_task(do_sleep(10.0))
    loop.create_task(print_input('Enter string: '))
    loop.run_forever()
```

It is supposed to print the sleep message every 10 seconds and the input string after entering some text and pressing enter. This works well when I enter some text and press enter before the sleep message is printed in between, see below:

1. Input string is entered before sleep message is printed.

```
Enter string: abc
Input was: abc
```

2. Input string is entered after sleep message is printed.

```
Enter string: Did sleep 10.00 seconds.
abc
Input was: abc
```

However, when the sleep message interferes while entering some text, everything before the sleep message seems to get lost.

```
Enter string: aDid sleep 10.00 seconds.
bDid sleep 10.00 seconds.
cDid sleep 10.00 seconds.
d
Input was: d
```

So is this the correct behavior and if yes, why does it behave like this?

Thanks!

issue_title: Boolean() validation woes
issue_body:
```
>>> from voluptuous import *
>>> schema = Schema({ Required('a'): Boolean() })
>>> schema({ 'a': [{'foo':'bar'}]})
{'a': True}
```

Is this expected behavior?  This is the shortened code snippet:
```python
>>> def Boolean(v):
...     if isinstance(v, basestring):
...         v = v.lower()
...         if v in ('1', 'true', 'yes', 'on', 'enable'):
...             return True
...         if v in ('0', 'false', 'no', 'off', 'disable'):
...             return False
...         raise ValueError
...     return bool(v)
```
It seems like it will only return `True` and `False` for the indicated values, and then return `True` for any populated value that is not of type `str` or `unicode`:

Dict:
```
>>> schema({ 'a': {'foo':'bar'}})
{'a': True}
```

Int:
```
>>> schema({ 'a': 99})
{'a': True}
```
Float:
```
>>> schema({ 'a': 123.45})
{'a': True}
```

It doesn't seem like a good idea to validate `Boolean()` if I can pass a list to the schema check and have it accidentally validate as True.

This can be partially solved by using `All(Any(str, unicode), Boolean())`, but this is not what I would _expect_ to need to do with the validator.  The problem I have is that after importing YAML data, `exclude: False` becomes `{'exclude': False}`, rather than `{'exclude': 'False'}`, which leads me to the current ugly, final solution:

```
Any(bool, All(Any(str, unicode), Boolean()))
```

There is nothing in your documentation that informs me of these hoops I have to jump through.  If I didn't read the code, I would have had no idea that it expects a string value.

As far as potential fixes go:
```
>>> def Boolean(v):
...     if isinstance(v, basestring):
...         v = v.lower()
...         if v in ('1', 'true', 'yes', 'on', 'enable'):
...             return True
...         if v in ('0', 'false', 'no', 'off', 'disable'):
...             return False
...         raise ValueError
...     elif isinstance(v, bool):
...         return bool(v)
...     else:
...         raise ValueError
...
>>> Boolean(1)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<stdin>", line 12, in Boolean
ValueError
>>> Boolean("1")
True
>>> Boolean("0")
False
>>> Boolean(True)
True
>>> Boolean(False)
False
>>> Boolean({'a':'b'})
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<stdin>", line 12, in Boolean
ValueError
```
It seems pretty straightforward to add the `elif` to test if it's a `bool` before attempting to return `bool(v)`.

It could also be modified to work with floats and ints (not just strings of `"1"`, and `"0"`:
```python
def Boolean(v):
    # Make floats into integers
    if isinstance(v, float):
        # Ensure that only 0.0 is 0
        v = 1.0 if v > 0 and v < 1 else v
        v = int(v)
    # Make integers strings for guaranteed comparisons 
    if isinstance(v, int):
        v = str(v)
    if isinstance(v, basestring):
        v = v.lower()
        if v in ('1', 'true', 'yes', 'on', 'enable'):
            return True
        if v in ('0', 'false', 'no', 'off', 'disable'):
            return False
        raise ValueError
    elif isinstance(v, bool):
        return bool(v)
    else:
        raise ValueError
```

I'm happy to submit this as a PR if you're satisfied this won't break existing, expected behavior.

issue_title: Problems installing voluptuous
issue_body:
At [Home Assistant](https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant) we use voluptuous and it is awesome. Thanks for all the work on this.

We've just released a new version which included an upgraded voluptuous. We went from 0.8.9 to 0.9.1.

We've had several users report that their installation of Home Assistant is hanging at the installation of voluptuous. The issue started with 0.9.0. I don't have a repo of the problem but we did find a workaround: installing `flake8` before installing `voluptuous` makes the problem disappear. 

Looking at the diffs between those versions, [these lines in setup.py](https://github.com/alecthomas/voluptuous/blob/master/setup.py#L54-L56) are probably the culprit:

```python
    setup_requires=[
        'flake8',
    ]
```

I did not want to submit a PR removing these lines without knowing the reasoning behind it. It looks to me that the lines can be removed because `tox` [is setup](https://github.com/alecthomas/voluptuous/blob/master/tox.ini#L24) to install the dependency.

issue_title: consider renaming the library
issue_body:
I didn't see anyone having raised this issue before, so I thought perhaps you might not realize: the word "voluptuous" in english has a heavily sexual connotation.  [The very first noun associated with it in the dictionary definition](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/voluptuous) is "nudes".  

[Using unnecessarily sexualized language can create an environment that is unduly hostile to women](http://geekfeminism.wikia.com/wiki/Sexualized_environment), even if this is not intended. Many of the biases against women that such language provokes are totally unconscious, so even if the intent is not harmful, it can be bad regardless.

Finally, the _very first_ thing in the README says "despite the name" so it seems that perhaps it's occurred to you that this isn't a great name for a library.

In summary: in the interests of creating a more inclusive community, please come up with a new name for this library.  Thank you for reading!

issue_title: bumpversion and setuptools have differing interpolation behavior
issue_body:
(Migrating from https://github.com/peritus/bumpversion/issues/181)

The interpolation behavior of config files by `bumpversion` and `setuptools` is not the same. `bumpversion` uses no interpolation (e.g. [`configparser.RawConfigParser`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/configparser.html#configparser.RawConfigParser)), but `setuptools` uses the default interpolation of [`configparser.ConfigParser`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/configparser.html#configparser.ConfigParser), which is "basic interpolation".

In general, this should not be an issue, but because `bumpversion` supports setting configuration in the `setup.cfg` file, options that might be interpolated by the parser would not cause an issue if they appear in the `.bumpversion.cfg`, but suddenly break the install/build process if they are moved to the `setup.cfg`.

For example, I had the following in my `.bumpversion.cfg` to automatically insert the date in my CHANGELOG along with the version.

```
[bumpversion:file:docs/source/changelog.rst]
search = XX-XX-XXXX v. X.X.X
replace = {now:%m-%d-%Y} v. {new_version}
```

When I moved this into my `setup.cfg`, `bumpversion` still works but the following happens when I try to run anything with `setup.py`.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
 ...
configparser.InterpolationSyntaxError: '%' must be followed by '%' or '(', found: '%m-%d-%Y} v. {new_version}'
```

The obvious solution of escaping the `%` so that they are not interpolated (e.g. `{now:%%m-%%d-%%Y}` causes `setuptools` to not fail, but then `bumpversion` inserts the literal string "`%%m-%%d-%%Y`" instead of formatting the date because the percent signs are not treated as needing escaping.

It turns out that other packages have run into this, like `pytest`: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/3062. [This `distutils` mailing thread](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/distutils-sig/2017-July/031091.html) also illustrates that the python devs are struggling with if this was a good design decision, but that's a whole other can of worms.

**My proposal**: When loading from `setup.cfg`, use `configparser.ConfigParser`, and when loading from `.bumpversion.cfg`, use `configparser.RawConfigParser`.

issue_title: SQLite gotcha
issue_body:
If you try to set a SQLAlchemy `DateTime` value using SQLite as the backing database, you will get
````
sqlalchemy.exc.StatementError
StatementError: SQLite Date type only accepts Python date objects as input.
````

Since you cannot really import `datetime` and set this in the json directly it be handled as the object is created

`````
import datetime
created = datetime.utcnow()
cod = Code(created=created)
db.session.add(cod)
db.session.commit()
``````

Or using a separate setter function.

Again this is a gotcha with SQLite that is up to you to either warn people about or provide a way for them to be set.

issue_title: Add black fmt: off tags to test n-dimensional arrays
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
The problem is that whenever we have a multidimensional array for test-cases, black formats them
"vertically", resulting to a very high LOC and uglier code. See [this issue](https://github.com/ambv/black/issues/440)

**To Reproduce**
```
$ black tests/
$ vim tests/backend/topology/test_von_neumann.py
```

Then check `test_delannoy_numbers` and observe how the n-dim array is formatted.

**Expected behavior**

It's better to retain the current formatting. 

**Environment (please complete the following information):**
 - OS: [e.g. Linux] Linux
 - Version [e.g. Ubuntu 16.04] Ubuntu 16.04
 - PySwarms Version [e.g. v.0.2.0] v.0.3.0
 - Python Version [e.g. 3.6.X] 3.6.X

**Additional context**

We can easily solve this by using  `fmt: off` followed by `# fmt: on` on specific parts of the code. However, we also need to identify parts of the code where we need to use this formatting.

issue_body:
../pages/models.py, around line number 500, below
```
    language = models.CharField(_('language'), max_length=5, blank=False)
```
Which could cause issue if >=django 1.8 used with ```zh-hans``` which has 7 characters instead of 5. So, to be safe I set it 10

issue_title: Environment variables should not have precedence over `Consul` constructor arguments
issue_body:
The token passed in as an argument in the constructor is overriden by the `CONSUL_HTTP_TOKEN` (if set): https://github.com/cablehead/python-consul/blob/764545c45df6d33a41eefc75cb1c68596042b7a0/consul/base.py#L311

The same is true with the host and port:
https://github.com/cablehead/python-consul/blob/764545c45df6d33a41eefc75cb1c68596042b7a0/consul/base.py#L297-L299

I would argue that this is not the behaviour most would expect. It would be better if the precedence order was `argument > env > default`.

issue_title: login redirect should work with null request parameters
issue_body:
I am using the Djinga templating engine (which processes both Django and Jinja templates)  so I am not sure if this behavior is reproducible either in the vanilla Django template or even Jinja2. The social.apps.django_app.context_processors.login_redirect context processor fails when rendering using the django.template.loader.render_to_string() function.

The reason is very simple: Djinga passes request=None to all context processors when render_to_string is called and login_redirect fails with this kind of input. I am not sure if Djinga is abusing an undocumented Django behavior or login_redirect is the one to blame. 

Anyway, the error can be fixed trivially by checking if request is None in the begining of the login redirect function.

issue_title: Pretty-print XML in debug output
issue_body:
Debugging would be more fun if XML in the DEBUG output was pretty-printed and syntax highlighted. We can create a custom `logging.Formatter` that does something like this in DEBUG mode:

```python
import io

from lxml.etree import parse, tostring
from pygments import highlight
from pygments.lexers import XmlLexer
from pygments.formatters import TerminalFormatter

def pprint(xml_str):
    return tostring(parse(
        io.BytesIO(xml_str)),
        xml_declaration=True,
        encoding='utf-8',
        pretty_print=True
    ).replace(b'\t', b'    ').replace(b' xmlns:', b'\n    xmlns:')

return highlight(pprint(xml_string), XmlLexer(), TerminalFormatter())
```

issue_title: your example is off
issue_body: You get the same model instance twice and save each and you get a concurrency error?  Why exactly?  You haven't changed any fields!  Optimistic concurrency means detecting changes to an object at save/commit that overwrite concurrent changes.  It generally requires keeping track of mutations of the object somehow or using a shadow object.  If this really works like the example it is broken by design.

issue_title: Cache option doesn't appear to do anything
issue_body:
I tried turning options.CACHE on, but it didn't appear to have any effect. I dug into cachecontrol, and from what I can tell, it won't cache anything unless the response has a Cache-Control header and either a nonzero max-age or an "Expires" header. neo4j (3.0.1) doesn't send either of these, so I don't think it will ever cache a response.

I can't think of any way to fix this in this project, so I've modified my copy of cachecontrol so it caches things without the headers. It might be worth removing the section on caching from the documentation though. If you agree I can submit a PR.

issue_title: Change directory structure
ZstdDecompressor stream_reader doesn't support readline()
issue_body: The documentation for ZstdCompressor explicitly says that readline() isn't supported because it makes no sense for compressed data. For Decompressor, this remark is missing - as it should be - but the function doesn't seem to be implemented and throws NotImplementedError.

issue_title: python-zstandard incompatible with pypy
issue_body:
Greetings,

I ran into another issue when I started using python-zstandard with pypy.  The module builds, but it fails at runtime because the bytes objects can't be resized.  My workaround has been to change decompress2 to return a list of strings (iterate over -- decompression, PyBytes_FromStringAndSize on the output, PyList_Append with the result, and Py_DECREF the result) instead of growing a single string.  I suspect this change makes things slower (not to mention incompatible), but it was worth it for me to get pypy working quickly.

issue_title: Python 3.2 compatibility
support numeric precision changes
Problem using UUID binary type with foreign key relationship between models
issue_body:
Running into a problem trying to use a binary type with a foreign key relationship between models. This stems from trying to use the UUIDType from SQLAlchemy-Utils and choosing the binary form, which uses a BINARY(16) type for the UUID column. This is using MariaDB. Relevant software and library versions are included at the end.

Here are the create table scripts:

``` mysql

MariaDB [flask_accounts_development]> show create table account\G;
*************************** 1. row ***************************
       Table: account
Create Table: CREATE TABLE `account` (
  `id` binary(16) NOT NULL,
  `username` varchar(64) NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  UNIQUE KEY `username` (`username`)
) ENGINE=InnoDB DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8
1 row in set (0.00 sec)

ERROR: No query specified
```

``` mysql
MariaDB [flask_accounts_development]> show create table address\G;
*************************** 1. row ***************************
       Table: address
Create Table: CREATE TABLE `address` (
  `id` int(11) NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `account_id` binary(16) NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  KEY `account_id` (`account_id`),
  CONSTRAINT `address_ibfk_1` FOREIGN KEY (`account_id`) REFERENCES `account` (`id`)
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=2 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8
1 row in set (0.00 sec)

ERROR: No query specified
```

Account and Address model definitions:

``` python
from app import db
from sqlalchemy_utils import UUIDType

class Account(db.Model):
    id = db.Column(UUIDType, primary_key=True)
    username = db.Column(db.String(64), unique=True, nullable=False)
    addresses = db.relationship('Address', backref=db.backref('account'), cascade='all, delete-orphan')

    def __init__(self, username):
        self.username = username

    def __repr__(self):
        return '<Account id=%r name=%r>' % (self.id, self.username)

class Address(db.Model):
    id          = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    account_id  = db.Column(UUIDType, db.ForeignKey('account.id'), nullable=False)

    def __repr__(self):
        return '<Address id=%r account_id=%r>' % (self.id, self.account_id.hex)
```

Code to exercise the issue:

``` python
from app.accounts.models import Address
from app import db
from app.accounts.models import Account
import uuid

account = Account(username='david')
account.id = uuid.uuid4()

db.session.add(account)
db.session.commit()

address = Address(account=account)

db.session.add(address)
db.session.commit()

account.addresses
```

Running this from a Flask shell, I get the following error trying to get the addresses for an account:

issue_title: Each solution has only one possible audio challenge, leading to possibility of a rainbow table type attack
issue_body:
Hi,

For any given input text, Flite produces the same output audio every time (when used with the same settings in the same environment with the same version, at least - and all of these things would be true for a single server). This is unlike the image captchas, in which a single input text can randomly produce one of many, many challenge images. Because of this, it is possible for an attacker to generate all possible audio challenges and store them for lookup later. This is especially easy since the challenges are only 4 letters long.

To solve this problem, there should be many different possible audio challenges for each solution, so that it becomes infeasible for an attacker to generate all possible challenges in a reasonable amount of time.

To demonstrate this issue, I have written a [proof of concept](https://github.com/appleorange1/django-simple-captcha-cracker-poc) targeting your test application, which you can run to confirm the issue. I include more details, and some potential ideas for solutions, in the [README](https://github.com/appleorange1/django-simple-captcha-cracker-poc/blob/master/README.md) for that project.

Thanks,

appleorange1
