issue_title: Certification: Output commissioning logs
issue_body:
Test harness requires a way to get commissioning logs, and retrieval some useful information for verification.

For certification test 8_x_x, harness will verify the following messages based on the logs: 
- JOIN_FIN.req
- JOIN_FIN.rsp
- JOIN_ENT.req
- JOIN_ENT.rsp

Since they are all sent in an encrypted DTLS ApplicationData message.

Logs of the upper four messages need to include following information:
- type (0: JOIN_FIN.req, 1: JOIN_FIN.rsp, 2: JOIN_ENT.req, 3: JOIN_ENT.rsp)
- direction (in/out)
- eui
- length
- tlvs payload

While the **type** is used to verify the present of messages, and **tlvs payload** is used to verify the present of tlvs (only verify Provisioning URL TLV for now, not present in test 8_1_1, should present in test 8_1_6).

issue_title: Stack reverts to non-volatile settings when changed by application
issue_body:
Recently #1586 has been applied to invalidate Operational Dataset in case one of change of some parameters like channel or PAN ID.

Unfortunatelly i can't see this behaviour using CLI example, at least in a way i would expect this to work:

```
> panid 0x1234
Done
> ifconfig up
Done
> thread start
Done
> state
leader
Done
> thread stop  
Done
> ifconfig down
Done
> channel
11
Done
> channel 14
Done
> ifconfig up
Done
> thread start

> channel
11  <-- Channel has been reverted
Done
```

I found that the problem is here:
https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/meshcop/dataset_manager.cpp#L191

`mLocal `is actually never invalidated due to result of `Compare `method. If this result is omitted, then channel does not revert to one set in non-volatile memory.

@jwhui Should we just add a new parameter or method that will not perform this comparison or do you see more sophisticated solution?

issue_title: Proposal: napalm library should abstract away internal files and directory structure
issue_body:
There are two fundamental ways that `__init__.py` can be used:

1. As a way to enable directory traversal and with some very minimal level of code definition.

2. As a method of hiding the internal directory structure and file names of a library (or some meaningful part thereof).

There is disagreement in the Python community on how `__init__.py` should be used. Some projects use only item 1; others use item 1 and 2.

I researched some various large Python projects yesterday and here were some results:

### Largely use` __init__.py` for only directory traversal and minimal code definiton:
- twisted
- pytest
- boto

### Use `__init__.py` for directory traversal and abstracting library internals
- requests
- flask
- django (somewhat but it wrapped things inside a setup() function and was still pretty minimal)
- paramiko

I also looked at lxml also but it was integrating to C libraries.
 

I am pretty strongly in favor that we should use `napalm/__init__.py` to abstract away the internal library directory names and file names. In principle, this is exactly the same issue that the NAPALM library itself was created for. 

Basically, if we do not abstract these away, then are directories and file names become a part of our programming interface. This results in it being difficult for us to change library internals. It also makes it more likely for us to break code for users that are using our code.

Here is a first pass at some things that should be in `napalm/__init__.py`:

```
from napalm.base import get_network_driver
from napalm._SUPPORTED_DRIVERS import SUPPORTED_DRIVERS

from napalm.base.base import NetworkDriver
from napalm.base.exceptions import ReplaceConfigException, MergeConfigException, \
            ConnectionClosedException, CommandErrorException

from napalm.base.utils import py23_compat  # noqa
from napalm.base import constants       # noqa
from napalm.base import helpers     # noqa
```

Here is a test repo where I made the changes also to the IOS driver. 

https://github.com/ktbyers/napalm/commit/2a7c2bef5caf038c62c16ce8a24d7a0b0a5d59e0

I am less concerned about changing internal library references. I view this as largely optional (as we can always change internal references at a later date without breaking the code of any library users).

### What about performance?
I don't think this has a meaningful impact on memory size as `get_network_driver` will load `napalm.base.__init__` which will load almost all these things anyways. If we want to improve memory usage then we should figure out a way to not generally import large external libraries (in cases where they are not used).

### As part of this we should ensure that it is backwards compatible with Salt, StackStorm2, Ansible, and Netbox (if we do the above): 

Basically we should ensure that nothing here breaks backwards compatibility with other major tools that rely on us. I think this can be accomplished essentially equivalently with what is required for reunification. In other words, it will be the same level of difficulty as the problem we already have.

### Why it matters now?
Once we tell people to update the code to the new reunified structure and how to do that...we are exceptionally unlikely to ask them to do this again. This will potentially start publishing library internals to end-users and as more and more users rely on these things...it will be harder and harder to change.

issue_title: LANGUAGE variable in Makefile elided by LANGUAGE environment variable
issue_body:
The LANGUAGE variable in the Makefile is elided by the LANGUAGE environment variable. 

On many systems (including Ubuntu and MSYS2), the LANGUAGE environment variable is defined, e.g. as "en_US" or something like that.

This leads to `make` producing output like this:

```
$ make
mkdir -p ./gens
protoc --proto_path=.:/usr/local/include --en_US_out=./gens --grpc_out=./gens --plugin=protoc-gen-grpc=/usr/local/bin/grpc_en_US_plugin google/api/annotations.proto
protoc-gen-en_US: program not found or is not executable
--en_US_out: protoc-gen-en_US: Plugin failed with status code 1.
Makefile:41: recipe for target 'google/api/annotations.pb.cc' failed
make: *** [google/api/annotations.pb.cc] Error 1
```

Changing the variable name in the Makefile to something different - anything not already defined in the environment - fixes the issue.

issue_title: Convert to a package directory structure
issue_body:
``cmd2`` has grown to the point where the **cmd2.py** file is over 4000 lines and with new features being added the overall code size is likely to grow substantially.

It has reached that point that ``cmd2`` would benefit by becoming a real Python package with multiple source code files.

For this to happen, we will need a **cmd2** directory in which all of the source code lives and there is a dunder init.py file to make it a package.  This article on [Structuring Your Project](http://docs.python-guide.org/en/latest/writing/structure/) has good basic advice on how to approach this.

issue_title: It would be useful to have more examples
issue_body: Adding more, better, and more complicated / in depth examples which demonstrate the wealth of features present in cmd2 and the various ways cmd2 can be used in an application would likely be most helpful for new users.

issue_title: Windows and async output
issue_body:
I am running into an issue on windows - when on windows and have tab completion enabled(i.e. in pyreadline), any async output (i.e. output sent DURING reading of a line) printed via `cmd.poutput()` is erased once you enter a line. If I set `use_rawinput` to false, the problem goes away, but so does tab completion functionality, which is not acceptable....

I worked around the original issue by buffering the output lines that pass through poutput and re-printing them once raw_input completes, which is hacky,  but works, but then I discover that the last line of the output is now being returned by `raw_input` instead of my actual line entered....

I realize it is most likely some pyreadline quirk, but is there a way to get both async output AND tab completion to work under windows? And is there a replacement for pyreadline in the works,  given that pyreadline seems to be abandoned?

Thanks,

issue_title: Support SparkPost EU
issue_body:
SparkPost has [announced SparkPost EU](https://www.sparkpost.com/blog/announcing-sparkpost-eu/), a version of their service hosted entirely in the European Union. Anymail should add a setting to allow using the EU service as an alternative to the US-based default.

It looks like this just involves pointing the SparkPost API at the EU [API endpoint](https://developers.sparkpost.com/api/index.html#header-api-endpoints). The `sparkpost.SparkPost` constructor has an undocumented [`base_uri` parameter](https://github.com/SparkPost/python-sparkpost/blob/v1.3.6/sparkpost/__init__.py#L18) that seems to allow this. 

The new Anymail setting should probably be SPARKPOST_API_URL (rather than _URI), paralleling similar settings in other backends.

issue_title: Pickle support type matching problem
Can't override method returning primitive value references
Kerberos supprt
issue_body:
Currently, djangoldapdb only supports anonymous and non-anonymous simple binds. The underlying python-ldap does support Kerberos (SASL GSSAPI) so we should do so, too.

There are at least two scenarios:

 1. The webserver hvaing a static Kerberos ticket
 2. The browser passing a Kerberos ticket through the webserver to Django

Both cases should be supported

issue_title: Support PyPy
issue_body:
We used to support pypy, but the introduction of psycopg2 support causes issues, so we have removed it.

We should be able to leverage psycopg2cffi as a drop in, but managing the intstall_requires needs some care. Also, the pytest-postgresql test plugin we use doesn't support using psycopg2cffi, so we'd need to fork/upstream a change that allows it to depend on/use pyscopg2cffi on pypy.

issue_title: Support git as a reverter layer?
issue_body:
The reverter and rollback commands are pretty unpleasant from a UX perspective right now. We could consider the option of working with server configs in git repos (either preexisting or make one for the user), which would allow a few things to be done better:

* Provide a rich command line for winding forward and back that people might already know
* Be able to record which changes were made by Certbot (indeed, which exact run of Certbot) and which changes were made by the user in between Certbot runs

issue_title: Signals during error handling
issue_body:
Despite me writing `certbot/error_handler.py` many months ago, it just surprised me. What surprised me is more or less documented below:

> Every registered function is attempted to be run to completion exactly once. If a registered function raises an exception, it is logged and the next function is called. If a (different) handled signal occurs while calling a registered function, it is attempted to be called again by the next signal handler.

I think not calling a registered function again if it just raised an exception makes sense. If it errored the first time, it's likely to error again. I'm not sure if calling the function again if it is interrupted by a signal, however, is the right behavior. Registered functions can change state and an interrupted function may leave something in an invalid state such that calling it again causes incorrect behavior.

For a real life example, `client.py` registers the method `_restart_and_rollback` with the `ErrorHandler`. This method rolls back one checkpoint and restarts the plugin. What if, however, a signal interrupts the method after rolling back the checkpoint. Calling it again will attempt to rollback another checkpoint which is incorrect.

I think we should call try and call a registered function at most once to avoid this problem.

issue_title: Typo in sent email for beta invite
issue_body:
Heya,

There is a small typo I noticed in the email you send

this:

```
./letsencrypt-auto certonly -a webroot --webroot-path /var/www/example \
  -d example.com -d www.example.com \
  -server https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/directory --agree-dev-preview
```

Should be this:

```
./letsencrypt-auto certonly -a webroot --webroot-path /var/www/example \
  -d example.com -d www.example.com \
  --server https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/directory --agree-dev-preview
```

The `--server` is missing one `-` .

issue_title: Domain in cli.ini breaks renew
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
openSUSE Leap 42.2

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
certbot-auto

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
certbot-auto renew --dry-run

Currently, the renew verb is capable of either renewing all installed certificates that are due to be renewed or renewing a single certificate specified by its name. If you would like to renew specific certificates by their domains, use the certonly command. The renew verb may provide other options for selecting certificates to renew in the future.

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
It should simulate to renew the certs.

## Here is a Certbot log showing the issue (if available):
2017-06-03 22:13:31,963:DEBUG:certbot.log:Exiting abnormally:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/letsencrypt", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/lib/python2.7/site-packages/certbot/main.py", line 742, in main
    return config.func(config, plugins)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/lib/python2.7/site-packages/certbot/main.py", line 692, in renew
    renewal.handle_renewal_request(config)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/lib/python2.7/site-packages/certbot/renewal.py", line 370, in handle_renewal_request
    raise errors.Error("Currently, the renew verb is capable of either "
Error: Currently, the renew verb is capable of either renewing all installed certificates that are due to be renewed or renewing a single certificate specified by its name. If you would like to renew specific certificates by their domains, use the certonly command. The renew verb may provide other options for selecting certificates to renew in the future.

## Here is the relevant nginx server block or Apache virtualhost for the domain I am configuring:
N/A

Further information
The reason for this error is a line in cli.ini:
domain = d1.example.com,d2.example.com

I added this line because of this "workaround" https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/set-default-cn-name-for-multiple-certificate/21625/4 mentioned in this issue: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/3698
I also found this issue about domains in cli.ini https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/4096

From https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/4096 it seems that the proper key in cli.ini is "domains" instead of "domain". The odd thing is, that I could get a cert with all my domains using "domain".
Unfortunately the proper key is undocumented in https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/examples/cli.ini

Either there is no error message about the wrong key or bug #4096 fixes only a part of the problem.

issue_title: Running certbot --nginx results in a config file with inconsistent indentation
Breaking extra "Listen 0.0.0.0:443" created in httpd.conf during renewal in certbot's apache plugin
issue_body:
I've run into this several times now on OpenSUSE 42.1, using apache 2.4. httpd.conf doesn't include `Listen` directives on its own but rather `Include /etc/apache2/listen.conf`.

What happens is that certbot, seemingly unaware of this, ends up adding this at the end of httpd.conf:
```
<IfModule mod_ssl.c>
Listen 0.0.0.0:443
Listen 0.0.0.0:443
</IfModule>
```

Note that it happens twice too (perhaps because I'm renewing a cert for 2 domains). Regardless, this breaks apache because it can't bind to port 443 more than once, so it doesn't start at all.

Sample command used:
`certbot --test-cert --break-my-certs --webroot --installer apache --webroot-path /srv/www/htdocs/site.com -d dev.site.com -d dev2.site.com --post-hook "/usr/sbin/rcapache2 reload; /usr/sbin/rcnginx reload"`

I believe this also happened in much simpler renewal commands too when I was testing, but, interestingly, not always - for example, it didn't happen during the initial certificate installation.

issue_title: Check untyped defs in the Nginx plugin
issue_labels: [{'id': 277080619, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20code%20health', 'name': 'area: code health', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 149596829, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': 'c61aff', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
This issue is for checking untyped definitions in the `certbot-nginx` module.

See explanation here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fp0tPP9bzYkUViTnOXAyoT_KfgTfs8HSpNMoU8Tm_SY/edit?usp=sharing

From http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/:

Add the `--check-untyped-defs` option to your mypy arguments, and get that running with no errors on the codebase. This option causes mypy to check every def in the codebase for internal consistency; mypy can detect many classes of bugs and mistakes in your codebase, without your having written a single type annotation!

In most cases, you’ll want to fix bugs and bad code as you go, but you can also use #type: ignore annotations or exclude files to defer issues. For example, we excluded all of Zulip’s tests at first, since they’re both lower value to type-check and had most of the monkey-patching and other questionable Python in the project. For Zulip, getting clean `--check-untyped-defs` output took me about 2 days of hard work, including merging fixes for about 40 issues in the Zulip codebase.

I spent another day or two generating nice reproducers for the mypy bugs I’d encountered and improving typeshed. Now that mypy is no longer in its infancy, mypy bugs are increasingly rare. But a large project should expect to encounter and fix typeshed issues (just submit a PR!).

Once `mypy --check-untyped-defs` passes on your codebase, you should lock down your progress by running the mypy checker in your CI environment.

Because mypy type annotations are optional, once you’ve done the setup above, you can start annotating your codebase at whatever pace you like. Over time, you’ll get the benefits of static types in the parts of the codebase that you’ve annotated, without needing to do anything special to the rest of the codebase (the wonders of gradual typing!). In the next section, I’ll discuss strategy for how to move the codebase towards being fully annotated.

issue_title: Get rid of cv2.cv in order to support OpenCV3
issue_body:
I'm developing using OpenCV3 (master, compiled from sources). MavProxy's map does not like OpenCV3, mainly because of the outdated `import cv2.cv as cv` (and the respective legacy C-bindings of OpenCV), as OpenCV3 [got rid of cv2.cv](http://stackoverflow.com/a/26859781/2559632).

I don't think that the C-bindings dependency is still necessary (open for discussion) and am currently working (on an almost done) PR which allows the use OpenCV3 alongside OpenCV2 for the `map module`.

issue_title: HttpAccessTokenRefreshError: invalid_grant: Token has been revoked
issue_body:
I noticed an issue where `context.credentials.get_access_token()` does not return the most recent oauth2client access token.

Steps to reproduce
1) Run datalab locally and sign in. Confirm that a BigQuery SQL query works
2) Remove the access which was granted to `Google Cloud SDK` in your [Google Account Security Settings](https://security.google.com/settings/security/secureaccount)
3) Confirm that a BigQuery SQL query now fails with error `invalid_grant: Token has been revoked.`
4) Token is now revoked. I sign in again in the same local datalab environment.
5) Since I just signed in, I expect BigQuery sql query will work with the new access token. I still get the error  `invalid_grant: Token has been revoked.`

```
>from datalab.context import Context
>context = Context.default().credentials.get_access_token()
HttpAccessTokenRefreshErrorTraceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-16-db0a2becc447> in <module>()
      1 from datalab.context import Context
      2 
----> 3 context = Context.default().credentials.get_access_token()

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/oauth2client/client.pyc in get_access_token(self, http)
    773             if not http:
    774                 http = httplib2.Http()
--> 775             self.refresh(http)
    776         return AccessTokenInfo(access_token=self.access_token,
    777                                expires_in=self._expires_in())

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/oauth2client/client.pyc in refresh(self, http)
    656                   request.
    657         """
--> 658         self._refresh(http.request)
    659 
    660     def revoke(self, http):

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/oauth2client/client.pyc in _refresh(self, http_request)
    861         """
    862         if not self.store:
--> 863             self._do_refresh_request(http_request)
    864         else:
    865             self.store.acquire_lock()

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/oauth2client/client.pyc in _do_refresh_request(self, http_request)
    930             except (TypeError, ValueError):
    931                 pass
--> 932             raise HttpAccessTokenRefreshError(error_msg, status=resp.status)
    933 
    934     def _revoke(self, http_request):

HttpAccessTokenRefreshError: invalid_grant: Token has been revoked.
```

I've confirmed that oauth2client returns the correct token, which is still valid. Here is the test code:

```
import oauth2client
auth_header = "Authorization:\ Bearer\ " + oauth2client.client.GoogleCredentials.get_application_default().get_access_token()[0]
!curl -H "Metadata-Flavor: Google" -H $auth_header https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v1/userinfo
```

It seems it is unlikely that users will hit this specific issue.

~~An additional possibility is that the access token expires. The user signs in successfully, but datalab is still using the expired access token.~~
This second scenario, access token expiration, may not be an issue based on the following [link](https://developers.google.com/adwords/api/docs/guides/authentication#access_token_expiration) that says access tokens will be refreshed automatically by oauth2client.

Currently the Rest API layer contains no logging calls at all. Solution: Add logging calls at least to the Rest API layer for at least long-running operations.

issue_title: Check if docker-py can be installed without docker.
issue_body: Currently `docker-py` is optional because it might require docker. However, sos would produce a warning message when docker related actions are imported if `docker-py` is not available. We need to make `docker-py` a required dependency if its installation does not rely on docker.

issue_title: Unify task execution
issue_body:
Right now tasks are executed in different ways,

1. Local jobs are executed by `multiprocessing` `subprocess`
2. Multiple jobs are executed by `multiprocessing` `pool` (async)
3. rq jobs are executed through rq job submission
4. celery jobs are executed through celery job submission
5. remote jobs are executed through saved task files.

With the introduction of new `sos execute task` command, I would propose that we execute all tasks using the `sos execute` command. That is to say, the subprocesses will not fork the parental process, and will all read from a job description file. This has the benefits of

1. Simplified code.
2. Easier testing because we know a job would run if it runs in one of the environments.
3. Easier to use a job pool (#400)
4. Easier cross platform because this might work better under windows (#374)

issue_title: Replacing our progress bar with tqdm
issue_body:
We have done a lot to use progress bar in a multiprocessing setting, but [tqdm](https://github.com/tqdm/tqdm) seem to [also have such a problem](https://github.com/tqdm/tqdm/issues/285). However, it makes sense to use tqdm, which is supposed to have less overhead (at least they take overhead seriously) than our progress bar.

Another side of the story is 'it is not broken, do not fix it'.

issue_title: revamp sos_run implementation (nested workflow)
issue_body:
Currently nested workflow is implemented as  a completely separate workflow. This has a number of problems, for example,

1. #400 number of processes can explode if we run multiple nested workflows.
2. Task manager: we will have multiple task managers for the same host, which can be problematic.
3. DAG. The DAGs will be separated into separate small DAGs which can not be optimized.

The proper way to implement `sos_run` seem to be creating a proxy process that executes the workflow, but

1. Use the master DAG to query for required steps to run.
2. Send steps to be executed to the master process that execute using the master process poll.
3. send tasks to the master process.

issue_title: List completed tasks.
issue_body: Right now when tasks are completed, we replace the task list with a message like "4 tasks completed", giving no chance for users to refer to completed task. It would be nice to keep task ids (perhaps shorter ones) with links to trigger `%taskinfo`.

issue_title: Data from new dataset is mixed up
issue_body:
### Steps to reproduce
1. Create a measurement and get a reference to its dataset
```
meas = Measurement()
meas.registerParameter(dac.ch1)  # register the first independent parameter
meas.registerParameter(dmm.v1, setpoints=(dac.ch1,))  # now register the dependent one

with meas.run() as datasaver:
    datasaver._dataset.add_metadata('database', 'database')
         
    for set_v in np.linspace(0, 25, 100):
        dac.ch1.set(set_v)
        get_v = dmm.v1.get()
        datasaver.addResult((dac.ch1, set_v),
                            (dmm.v1, get_v))

    dataid = datasaver.id  # convenient to have for plotting

// why underscore the reference? I my view its perfectly reasonable to get the dataset directory from the datasaver.
ds = datasaver._dataset

```
2a. Get the independent axes
`ds.get_setpoints(ds.get_parameters()[0].name)`
2b. 
`ds.get_setpoints(ds.get_parameters()[1].name)`

3a. Get the (in)dependent axes
`ds.get_data(ds.get_parameters()[0].name)`
3b. 
`ds.get_data(ds.get_parameters()[1].name)`

### Expected behavior
2. Get a **single non-nested list** of the independent parameter setpoint, i.e. [s1, s2, s2, ....]
3. Get a **single non-nested list** of the (dependent) parameter values, i.e. [s1, s2, s2, ....]

### Actual behavior
2a. Fails with 
`ValueError: Parameter dac_ch1 has no setpoints.`, which is ok, i guess?
2b. Returns 
Got a **list in a list in a list**, i.e. [[[0, s2, s2, ....., 25]]], which does correspond to _dac.ch1.set(set_v)_

3a. Returns 
.. a **list in a list** i.e. [0, s2, s3, .... 25], which seems to correspond to _dac.ch1.set(set_v)_
3b. Returns 
.. a **list in a list** i.e. [4.911194874618999, s2, s3, .... 0.15320196425034593], which seems to correspond to _dmm.v1, get_v_, instead of _dac.ch1.set(set_v)_ unlike before under **point2b**.

The behavior of ds.get_data_ is consistent with _ds.get_values_ and with what I would expect.

### Feature request
1. Get the independent parameter setpoint values without knowing the name or using something like ParamSpec
`
// axes_id is the position of the parameter in the tuple given to datasaver.addResult()
ds.get_axes(int axes_id)
// or
ds.get_setpoints(int axes_id)
`
1. Get the dependent parameter values without knowing the name or using something like ParamSpec.
`
data = ds.get_data()
//data contains only the dependent (i.e. measured) parameters...
`

### System
**operating system**

**qcodes branch**
feature/dataset

**qcodes commit**

issue_title: Investigate Incorrect/Removed Account Endpoints
issue_body:
#179 Added a script to check docstrings. It resulted in many good fixes, but there are still a few outstanding issues:

> canvasapi/canvas.py create_account docstring refers to method.accounts.create in https://canvas.instructure.com/doc/api/accounts.html, not found

> canvasapi/account.py create_account docstring refers to method.accounts.create in https://canvas.instructure.com/doc/api/accounts.html, not found

The above methods (`Account.create_account` and `Canvas.create_account`) refer to endpoints that no longer appear in the documentation. Determine if the endpoints are still valid. If they are, find a way to have the validation script ignore these two cases. If they are not, consider removal.

> canvasapi/account.py close_notification_for_user docstring DELETE /api/v1/accounts/:account_id/users/:user_id/account_notifications/:id not found in https://canvas.instructure.com/doc/api/account_notifications.html#method.account_notifications.user_close_notification (found ['DELETE /api/v1/accounts/:account_id/account_notifications/:id'])

> canvasapi/account.py get_user_notifications docstring GET /api/v1/accounts/:account_id/users/:user_id/account_notifications not found in https://canvas.instructure.com/doc/api/account_notifications.html#method.account_notifications.user_index (found ['GET /api/v1/accounts/:account_id/account_notifications'])

The above methods (`Account.close_notification_for_user` and `Account.get_user_notifications`) seem to have changed to no longer take in a user. It looks like it now applies the changes only to the current user. Determine if this is indeed the case and adjust the method as necessary.

Once the above methods are fixed, add the docstring validation script to the Travis build as a required step. This will help prevent future docstring issues from cropping up.

### Action Items

- [ ] Check and fix `Account.create_account`
- [ ] Check and fix `Canvas.create_account`
- [ ] Check and fix `Account.close_notification_for_user`
- [ ] Check and fix `Account.get_user_notifications`
- [ ] Add docstring validation script to Travis build

issue_title: Failures during update do not fail the update
issue_body:
Copied from https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/633.

There is a failure midway through, but then we continue to process resource deletes. Pretty sure we didn't used to do this, and although it's not obviously wrong (and in fact nice in some cases), it's not clear its the safest thing to do when there is a failure. Was this an intentional change?

issue_title: Segfault on osx
issue_body:
The following simple code leads to a segfault on osx.

import ray
ray.init()
ray.put("k")

I do see the builds passing so it may be a user-specific issue.

issue_title: tune.run_experiments local_dir with tilde (~) is interpreted as relative path
issue_body:
### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: macOS High Sierra 10.13.1
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: pip binary
- **Ray version**: 0.4.0
- **Python version**: Python 3.6.5 :: Anaconda, Inc.

### Describe the problem
The following example considers `local_dir` to be relative to the directory where I run the script, whereas I believe it should consider it to be in the home directory. As an example, if I run the script in `/Users/username/ray_local_dir_test`, my results get written into `/Users/username/ray_local_dir_test/~/ray_results/my_experiment` instead of `/Users/username/ray_results` as I would expect.

This also confuses the `ray.tune.log_sync.get_syncer` to not correctly modify the remote_dir and thus all the files are written directly to the `upload_dir` instead of e.g. `upload_dir/upload_dir_test/my_experiment/my_func_0_alpha=0.2,beta=2_2018-05-06_15-13-31lkpz7e9q`.

```python
import ray
from ray import tune


def my_func(config, reporter):  # add the reporter parameter
    import time, numpy as np
    i = 0
    while True:
        reporter(timesteps_total=i, mean_accuracy=i ** config['alpha'])
        i += config['beta']
        time.sleep(.01)


tune.register_trainable('my_func', my_func)
ray.init(resources={'cpu': 1})


tune.run_experiments({
    'my_experiment': {
        'run': 'my_func',
        'trial_resources': {'cpu':1},
        'stop': { 'mean_accuracy': 100 },
        'config': {
            'alpha': tune.grid_search([0.2, 0.4]),
            'beta': tune.grid_search([2]),
        },
        'local_dir': '~/ray_results',
    }
})
```

Happy to submit a PR to fix this. I guess the `upload_dir` should be run through `os.path.expanduser` at some point, maybe in `Trial.__init__`. Does that sound correct?

issue_title: [tune] Improve tune Python API support for custom trainable classes
issue_body:
The high-level `run_experiments()` and `generate_trials()` APIs do not currently work for custom functions / classes defined directly in Python. This forces the user to use the lower-level `Trial`/`TrialRunner` API, which doesn't help with variant generation.

This can be fixed by allowing the user to pass in custom python functions / classes to run_experiments / generate_trials.

tldr, need to make sure the TODOs work below:

I want to tune a | from | This is how it's done
-- | -- | --
Custom function | Python | TODO
Custom function | Command-line | ./tune.py -f <script_runner_conf>
Custom trainable class | Python | TODO
Custom trainable class | Command-line | TODO
RLlib agent w/gym env | Python | call run_experiments()
RLlib agent w/gym env | Command-line | ./train.py --alg=<alg> --env=<env>
RLlib agent w/custom env | Python | TODO
RLlib agent w/custom env | Command-line | TODO

cc @richardliaw

issue_title: Allow fractional millisecond timeout for waits
issue_body:
Currently the below fails.

To reproduce:

```python
import ray
import time
ray.init()
@ray.remote
def f():
  time.sleep(5)
ray.wait([f.remote()], timeout=0.1)
```

issue_title: Renamed function in ProximalLossPolicy breaks PolicyGradient
issue_body:
#849 renamed the function in [this line](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/e1831792f846dd0869b472e8b932f6b677ed0ca2/python/ray/rllib/policy_gradient/loss.py#L85) from `compute_actions` to `compute`.

When the function is [called from policy_gradient.py](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/e1831792f846dd0869b472e8b932f6b677ed0ca2/python/ray/rllib/policy_gradient/policy_gradient.py#L270) the function is now called with the wrong name and it causes an error.

I fixed it by just changing the function name back to `compute_actions` which works for my use case, but I didn't look into whether anything else calls the function with the new name.

Relevant stacktrace:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "pong_model_container.py", line 33, in <module>

  File "pong_model_container.py", line 30, in test

AttributeError: 'ProximalPolicyLoss' object has no attribute 'compute_actions'

Hi, I'm having this rendering error in a django form:

![screen shot 2016-09-16 at 00 05 02](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3653680/18576172/c4443d2a-7ba4-11e6-8d9c-f40dcc9f6e1a.png)

As you can see, the error makes labels clash with next form field, and changes the vertically aligned radio buttons to horizontal.

This is the form:

``` python
class MailServiceRenewalForm(forms.Form):

    SUBSCRIPTION_TYPES = (

I'm using this to render the form: `{{ form|crispy }}`. And if I don't use crispy at all (`{{ form }}`) the form will look good as well.

Thanks for the help, I want to use last version since it improves support for Django 1.9.

issue_title: URL building in plugins
issue_body:
### Checklist

- [ ] This is a bug report.
- [ ] This is a feature request.
- [X] This is a plugin (improvement) request.
- [X] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description

Many plugins manipulate URLs to retrieve the underlying stream url.
An example: https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/b839cfd76b63eec3646a58ca19d7be560c422592/src/streamlink/plugins/dailymotion.py#L235

While uploading the previous version of streamlink to Debian, Paul Wise suggested to improve URL handling using something to manipulate URL safely which would understand, for example, query parameters and encode them.


What do you think about adding a URL utility class that would take care of URL handling ?
I though about something like `string.format` or `%` but urlencoding every parameters. I'm not sure though that this URL handling improvement can be used in every URL manipulations.

As an example, if the first string contains query parameters, the concatenation with `/videos` won't work (the result would be like `https://dailymotion.com/first_path?param1=data/videos`).
In this very case, `USER_INFO_URL` does not contains query parameters, but `username` might contains special characters.

[Love Streamlink? Please consider supporting our collective. Thanks!](https://opencollective.com/streamlink/donate)

issue_title: Playing music causes Python to use all CPU memory
issue_body:
Hello,

I will try and explain my issue in as great detail as possible. I've owned a Red bot, largely without issue for over 7 months on the same VPS server (important later on).

Recently, about 2 days ago I did an update as I saw this in the Red Discord server announcements: "Updating the requirements from the launcher should fix the status issue". So as usual, I updated pip, updated Red and updated requirements. I then proceeded to install basic requirements.

Everything went fine, the bot started up as normal and worked absolutely perfectly.

Users notified me that there was lag when music was playing, I started trying to figure out what was causing lag and I noticed the CPU usage for Python (It's a windows server VPS) was bouncing around, from 1% CPU to 80% to 45% and so on and so forth.

I spoke to some of the help staff and other members, updated all cogs, updated the audio requirements, tried everything. 

I've tried changing the music format from FFMPEG to AVCON (I may misspell these as i'm not familiar with them).

I've also unloaded ALL cogs, even the core ones and restarted the bot, loaded only Audio, tried to play music, and again, the CPU memory starts to flutter and go all over the place.

I hope i've given enough detail, I will keep an eye on this thread and provide additional details if required.

Thanks in advance.

issue_title: Allow app to specify outputs
issue_body:
Consider the following example:
```
In [1]: !cat first.txt
foo

In [2]: !cat test.sh
cat $1 > second.txt
```

### current version
```
@bash_app
def with_parsl(inputs=[], outputs=[]):
    return 'bash test.sh {inputs[0]}'
```
This app definition does not say anything about the expected output. You would call this and get the output as:
```
f = with_parsl(inputs=[File('first.txt')], outputs=[File('second.txt')])
out_file = f.outputs[0]
```
_Note that the caller of the app needs to know what the expected output of the app is-- I think that should be the app's job!_

### proposed version
I propose allowing this syntax (retaining the current `outputs=[]` behavior for cases where you want to produce an arbitrary number of files to be determined at runtime):
```
@bash_app
def with_parsl(file):
    return 'bash test.sh {file}', File('second.txt')
```
The return value can be a tuple of arbitrary length; the first value is the bash string to execute, and any subsequent values are outputs. You would call this as:
```
f, out_file = with_parsl(File('first.txt'))
```

The key differences in the proposed version are
1) We scan args and keyword args for `File`s and call `stage_in` on them as well as on entries in `inputs`. This allows the expected input to be documented in the app itself (for cases where an arbitrary number of inputs to be determined at runtime isn't needed). 
2) The expected output of the app is documented in the app itself (for cases where an arbitrary number of outputs isn't needed).

This has come up for me while working on swag-- there are a number of bash apps which run executables which create many hardcoded outputs, and it's annoying that the app can't specify them.

issue_title: Composable Operators
issue_body:
There was an off-topic discussion in #3 about what the right level of abstraction is regarding ONNX operators. I'd like to further that discussion here and propose a potential solution.

This classic tradeoff was summarized by @soumith as:
> Side 1: a mathematical IR, so that things are extremely expressive (also requested by the compiler efforts)
Side 2: vendors (particularly mobile vendors) implementing the operators in the IR efficiently

This tension between higher/coarser (side 2) and lower/finer (side 1) levels of abstraction is real and valid, but why not enable both levels to be represented simultaneously in a single data flow graph representation? By enabling operators to (optionally) be defined by other operators, it gives the mobile vendor the choice to match on the coarse operators (eg: SELU) and ignore the implementation of SELU provided for nascent backends.

Then the set of non-primitive operators can be represented in a 'standard library' of reference implementations using primitive operators (in addition to or in lieu of the C++ reference implementations), which can evolve at a more rapid pace from the primitive operators (mirroring something like libc++/libc and syscall interfaces).

Additionally, this enables a lifecycle for operators to make their way from researchers to hand tuned kernels: A researcher creates a new operator and defines it using ONNX primitives, this allows for all frameworks to implement this operator at some (potential) loss of performance immediately, and then once this operator becomes popularized, it can be matched by name directly.

This parallels the transition from assembly programming to higher (C) languages that enabled procedural programming and libraries, only in this case the domain of operators is more constrained, so it is feasible for backends to pattern match on functions as well as intrinsics.

Another nice aspect of this design is that these composed operators are also useful for representing RNN cells, fused kernels, and increasing levels of abstraction that we see in DL topologies (actor, critic, generator, discriminator, etc), that will potentially be valuable for selecting/addressing regions of computation for manipulation (take all weights from 'generator' and quantize them for this backend).

issue_title: Gemm operator doesn't support optional C matrix
issue_body: There could conceivably be cases of fully connected layers without biases, in which case it would be silly to create an MxN matrix of zeros, or one broadcastable to MxN, just to satisfy the non-optional nature of C. Although it's uncommon for frameworks to support a case like that, the frameworks do support such cases (`Linear(bias=False)` in PyTorch, `FullyConnected(no_bias=True)` in MxNet, etc.). It seems that even if it's not a very common, it's possibly useful if BatchNorm is used with FullyConnected since then the bias is not necessary.

issue_title: Is pow's test case wrong?
issue_body:
https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/6270bb68c54647f6c9ca56d640a73fe2dec2ad80/onnx/backend/test/case/node/pow.py#L49-L60

The case above seems wrong.
As [docs](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#pow) says, 
```
If necessary the right-hand-side argument will be broadcasted to match the shape of left-hand-side argument. 
When broadcasting is specified, the second tensor can either be of size 1 (a scalar value), or having its shape as a contiguous subset of the first tensor's shape. 
The starting of the mutually equal shape is specified by the argument "axis", and if it is not set, suffix matching is assumed. 1-dim expansion doesn't work yet.
```

So in this case, shape of `x` is (2, 3), `axis=0`, then shape of `y` should be in `((2,), (2,3))`.
But shape of `y = np.array([2, 3, 4]).astype(np.float32)` is (3,).

Did I miss something?
If not, I will create a pr to fix this.

>>> Literal('2008-12-18',
datatype=URIRef('http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#date'))
rdflib.Literal('2008-12-18', language=None,

Literal's __repr__ was changed from language to lang in r1139; which is now consistent with the __init__ method. 
The __repr__ no longer includes lang or datatype if they are None. See r1470.

issue_title: Paver-minilib.zip changing by each sdist
issue_body:
Paver-minilib.zip file content is changing by each sdist task call, because the file modification time is always updated
in the PKZip header. There was no such behavior in previous versions.
I use git and I don't know if file change is important or just time update.

Version: Paver 1.0.5
